title: Kitesurfing the Meltemi — Greek Islands Wind Guide
Kitesurfing the Mistral — South France Wind Guide 2026
meta_title: Mistral Wind Kitesurfing France 2026 | Surf Store
meta_description: Master the mistral wind for kitesurfing in France. Learn gear selection, technique tips, and safety. Find it at Surf Store.
excerpt: The mistral wind brings powerful, consistent conditions to Southern France. Learn how to harness this famous wind safely and effectively.
body_html:
Understanding These Conditions

The mistral wind is one of Europe's most celebrated and feared winds for water sports. This cold, northwesterly wind funnels down the Rhône Valley in Southern France, particularly around areas like Lake Garda, the Côte d'Azur, and Provence. What makes the mistral wind kitesurfing France so distinctive is its reliability and power: gusts regularly exceed 30 knots, with sustained winds holding steady in the 18–28 knot range.

The mistral typically arrives in winter and spring, lasting anywhere from three days to two weeks. It arrives suddenly, builds rapidly, and can create choppy seas with steep wave faces. Because the wind blunnels through valleys before reaching the water, you'll often experience violent gusts and lulls. The temperature drops noticeably—sometimes 10°C in a single day—making this a serious wind that demands respect and preparation.

Best Gear for These Conditions

For the mistral wind kitesurfing France, you need smaller kites and boards built for power and control. Most riders drop one or two sizes down from their usual arsenal. In 25–28 knot gusts, a 12m² kite is standard for experienced riders; in lighter mistral days (18–22 knots), a 14m² works well. Beginners should stay ashore or seek coaching until they're confident with a 7m² or 9m² in choppy water.

We recommend the Duotone Rebel or Cabrinha Switchblade for mistral conditions—both excel in gusty, inconsistent wind and deliver responsive turning. For a directional board, choose something between 36–42 litres with a narrower stance; the JP Australia Pro Wave is ideal if you're looking for something that carves and holds in offshore chop.

Wetsuits are non-negotiable. The mistral brings cold water and air; we recommend a 4/3mm or 5/4mm suit. The ION Onyx Winter offers exceptional thermal protection with sealed seams—crucial when you're spending hours in near-freezing conditions. Gloves and a hood are equally important; numb hands mean poor technique and safety risks.

Technique Tips

The mistral wind's gusty nature demands constant adjustment. Keep your weight low and centred; don't lean back into the kite during lulls or you'll lose edge control when the next gust hits. Use a narrower, more aggressive grip on the bar—you'll need fine control in a kite that wants to pivot.

Look for smoother pockets near headlands or between islands where the mistral wind kitesurfing conditions calm slightly. Avoid open, exposed water where gusts are fiercest. Practise your self-rescue frequently; if you fall, stay calm and work methodically to get the kite back up. The cold water works against you, so prevent capsizes by staying centred and reading the wind's behaviour.

When riding waves, stay closer to shore. The mistral wind kitesurfing France creates unpredictable wave sets—some beautiful, others close-outs. Short, sharp manoeuvres beat long, flowing turns; save the stylish carving for calmer days.

Safety Checklist

  • Check the forecast obsessively. Mistral predictions are reliable, but intensity varies. If gusts exceed 30 knots and you're new to strong wind, stay onshore.
  • Wear a helmet and impact vest. Cold water shock and choppy conditions increase the risk of disorientation and injury.
  • Never kite alone. Mistral sessions should include at least two other riders or an observer on shore.
  • Use a hydration vest with a knife. Cold exhaustion sets in fast; stay hydrated and carry a line-cutting blade in case of emergency.
  • Dress for water immersion. Cold-water protocols apply: 5/4mm wetsuit, gloves, hood, and booties are standard.
  • Avoid kelp and weeds. Mistral conditions often churn up debris; entanglement is a real risk.
  • Know your exit. Identify a safe beach or launch point before you head out. Mistral wind kitesurfing France demands clean, protected water nearby.

Our Gear Recommendations at Surf Store

At Surf Store, we've built our inventory around European conditions like the mistral. For mistral wind kitesurfing France, start with a Duotone Rebel 12m²—it's forgiving in gusts and rewarding once you dial in your technique. Pair it with a JP Australia Pro Wave 42L board for stability and response in chop. Finally, grab a ION Onyx 5/4mm Winter Wetsuit to stay warm and safe. All three are in stock and shipped across Europe. Visit Surf Store to check sizes and pricing, or contact our team for personalised advice based on your experience level and location.

title: Kitesurfing the Tramontane — Roussillon Wind Guide
meta_title: Tramontane Wind Kitesurfing France 2026 Guide
meta_description: Master the tramontane wind for kitesurfing in France. Learn conditions, gear choices, technique, and safety. Shop expert equipment at Surf Store.
excerpt: The tramontane is France's most powerful and consistent wind for kitesurfing, blowing cold and hard down the Rhône valley and across the Mediterranean coast.
body_html:
Understanding These Conditions

The tramontane wind is one of Europe's most dramatic and reliable thermal winds, and it transforms French Mediterranean and southern Rhône valley spots into world-class kitesurfing territory. This strong northwesterly current develops when cold continental air masses collide with warm Mediterranean air, creating a pressure gradient that funnels winds down established valleys and across open water at remarkable consistency.

If you're chasing the tramontane wind kitesurfing France, expect sustained speeds of 25–35 knots, with gusts regularly hitting 40+ knots. The wind typically arrives in spring and autumn, though winter months can also deliver excellent sessions. What makes the tramontane wind special for riders is its reliability: it can blow for days on end, creating multi-day sessions without the waiting around that plagues most European spots. The sea state responds predictably too—you'll encounter organised swell patterns rather than chaotic chop, especially on the coast south of Montpellier and around the Camargue.

The wind's character shifts with location. Inland on the Rhône, thermal heating intensifies afternoon gusts. On the coast, morning sessions are often lighter; by midday the tramontane has built to its strongest, maintaining power until late afternoon. Cold air pouring from the northwest remains stable, so you won't experience the thermal collapse that kills other valley winds by evening.

Best Gear for These Conditions

Strong, sustained winds demand kites built for control and response rather than pure float. In 25–32 knot tramontane conditions, a 13m or 15m freestyle/freeride kite from brands like Duotone or Cabrinha becomes your workhorse. These sizes deliver the responsive bar feel you need in gusty air, and they're forgiving when the wind suddenly picks up to 38 knots. Many experienced riders keep both sizes in their quiver to handle the full range.

For heavier wind days pushing 35+ knots, drop to a 11m or smaller. A kite with progressive leading edge and reinforced frame (such as the Duotone Rebel or Cabrinha Drifter) resists deformation under pressure and holds its shape through severe gusts.

Board choice matters significantly. The tramontane wind often creates choppy conditions close to shore, so a 136–146cm freestyle board with moderate rocker and durability wins over delicate wave shapes. JP Australia and Fanatic both produce solid freeride and freestyle boards suited to this punishment. If you're heading to flatter lagoon spots like Leucate or Gruissan, stay with 140cm or smaller; rougher coastal water demands something with slightly more forgiveness.

Wetsuits are non-negotiable. Spring and autumn tramontane sessions run 12–16°C water temperature, requiring a 4/3mm or 5/4mm suit with sealed seams. ION's Seek Amp 4/3 offers flexibility and durability without bulk, keeping you mobile during long sessions. Winter tramontane pushes water below 10°C, justifying a 6/5mm hooded suit.

Technique Tips

Riding the tramontane wind kitesurfing France demands respect for gust management. Keep your kite positioned forward (1 o'clock to 11 o'clock) rather than high overhead—this flattens the power curve and lets you absorb sudden wind spikes without being catapulted. Lower kite position also improves bar response in choppy water.

Lean back harder than usual. The consistent pressure from the tramontane rewards aggressive edge pressure and body rotation; you'll find yourself carving through rough conditions more easily if you commit fully to heel-side edges. Stay low in your stance and flex your knees to absorb chop.

Watch your drift. The tramontane funnel creates lateral wind shear, especially near the Rhône mouth. Your kite might feel stronger on starboard than port, or wind pressure might shift across your session. Mark an exit point before you launch and keep an eye on the shoreline to ensure you're not being blown offshore.

Safety Checklist

  • Gust management: Always wear a quality impact vest (ION makes excellent options). Gusty thermal winds can generate unexpected power spikes that throw you hard.
  • Offshore risk: The tramontane regularly pushes riders seaward. Confirm wind direction before launch and plan your exit route. Bring a buddy or inform someone ashore of your session window.
  • Equipment failure: Strong, consistent wind stresses gear relentlessly. Check bridle line integrity and bladder condition before each session. Carry a repair kit if you're more than a 10-minute swim from shore.
  • Hypothermia: Cold continental air means water temperature drops unexpectedly. Dress for conditions 5°C colder than the forecast suggests and limit session duration on your first winter tramontane day.
  • Launch and landing: Do not attempt unassisted launches in 30+ knot tramontane. Wait for a buddy or spend the extra money on a launch service at organised spots like Leucate or Port Saint-Louis.

Our Gear Recommendations at Surf Store

For tackling French tramontane wind kitesurfing conditions, we stock the exact equipment that performs. The Duotone Rebel kite range (13m and 15m) is our top recommendation—proven durability and predictable handling in sustained 25–35 knot gusts. Pair it with a Fanatic Freeride board (140cm) for responsive turning without sacrificing forgiveness in choppy water. Add an ION Seek Amp 4/3mm wetsuit for spring and autumn sessions, and you're fully prepared for the tramontane.

Shop these essentials at Surf Store and benefit from our expert team's real-world experience riding the tramontane. We're based in Slovenia with daily insight into European wind patterns and can advise you on spot-specific conditions and gear matching.

title: Kitesurfing the Levante — Tarifa East Wind Guide
meta_title: Levante Wind Tarifa Kitesurfing Guide 2026
meta_description: Master the Levante at Tarifa with expert tips on wind patterns, gear selection, and technique. Shop kites and wetsuits at Surf Store.
excerpt: The Levante wind at Tarifa brings powerful, consistent easterly conditions beloved by kitesurfers seeking reliable performance and skill progression.
body_html:
Understanding These Conditions

The Levante wind at Tarifa is an easterly wind that funnels through the Strait of Gibraltar, creating one of Europe's most consistent and powerful wind windows. This thermal and pressure-driven phenomenon typically builds from late morning and peaks in the afternoon, offering sustained 15–25 knot sessions with gusts reaching 30+ knots on stronger days. The Levante brings warm, dry air from the Sahara and is most predictable between spring and early autumn, though it can appear year-round.

What makes the Levante wind at Tarifa kitesurfing so appealing is its reliability and clean, organised swell. Unlike Atlantic swells that can create choppy conditions, the Levante generates predictable wave patterns along Tarifa's beaches—particularly at Punta Paloma and Los Lances. The wind tends to blow side-onshore, meaning you get both steady breeze and manageable wave height. Early morning glassy conditions quickly give way to texture as thermals build, then solid afternoon winds that last until sunset.

Best Gear for These Conditions

For levante wind Tarifa kitesurfing, your quiver needs flexibility because wind strength varies significantly across sessions. Most riders keep a 14m and 17m kite in rotation. On lighter Levante days (12–16 knots), a 17m or 19m is essential; a 14m works perfectly for the 18–25 knot sweet spot that dominates typical sessions. For heavier gusts, many drop to a 12m. We recommend the Duotone Rebel series—these kites are forgiving in gusty conditions and excel in side-onshore winds common at Tarifa.

Board selection should favour directional or freestyle boards in the 36–42 litre range. The choppier afternoon texture means you'll want drive and response rather than pure wave-riding softness. Look at Fanatic Skate or Nobile NHP models—they handle Levante's choppy texture beautifully while remaining accessible for progression riders.

Wetsuits are critical. Early morning sessions require a 4/3mm or 5/4mm hooded suit; by afternoon, a 3/2mm often suffices thanks to Saharan warmth. We stock ION Seek Amp 4/3mm hooded wetsuits with chest zips for easy entry after your first session, and their thermal lining keeps you comfortable during long Levante marathons.

Technique Tips

The Levante's side-onshore direction demands good edge control and body positioning. Because gusts can spike suddenly, keep your bar pressure light and stay centred over the board—don't chase wind window extremes. Use the thermals to your advantage: morning sessions are cleaner for tricks and progression; afternoon sessions offer more power for jumps and waves.

When riding waves in Levante conditions, angle your board slightly upwind to maintain control during gust cycles. The wind often lulls between thermal pulses, so expect rhythm changes every 30 seconds. Tuck your kite lower in the window during stronger gusts to maintain control without losing height.

Levante generates organised, rolling waves—not huge, but consistent. Use the kite's pendulum motion to build speed across the face, then unweight at the lip. This isn't big-wave riding; it's technical wave-play that rewards precision.

Safety Checklist

  • Gust management: Levante gusts can spike 5–10 knots without warning. Ride one size larger than you think you need and focus on bar control rather than size.
  • Offshore wind risk: Afternoon Levante can turn slightly offshore depending on Tarifa beach location. Never venture far seaward; stay within rescue range of the beach.
  • Crowding: Tarifa attracts global kitesurfers during Levante season. Check beach density and agree on clear zones with other riders before launching.
  • Sun and dehydration: Saharan air and long afternoon sessions lead to rapid dehydration. Drink constantly between sessions; sunscreen is essential.
  • Launch and landing: Afternoon thermals create wind shear near the beach. Launch early, land with a buddy, and never launch alone during high Levante days.

Our Gear Recommendations at Surf Store

Duotone Rebel 17m — A forgiving, powerful kite that handles Levante's gust cycles brilliantly. Its stable bar feel and responsive turning make it ideal for both progression and advanced riders tackling Tarifa's variable wind. Find it at Surf Store.

Fanatic Skate 39L — Directional, responsive, and built for choppy conditions. This board excels in Levante's textured afternoon wind and transitions seamlessly from tricks to small-wave riding. Shop now at Surf Store.

ION Seek Amp Hooded 4/3mm — Essential for early-morning Tarifa sessions when water temps are cool despite warm air. The hooded design and thermal lining keep you comfortable during extended sessions. Find it at Surf Store.

title: Kitesurfing the Poniente — Tarifa West Wind Guide
meta_title: Poniente Wind Tarifa Kitesurfing Guide 2026
meta_description: Master the Poniente wind at Tarifa with our expert kitesurfing guide. Learn conditions, gear, and technique tips. Shop now at Surf Store.
excerpt: The Poniente wind brings consistent westerly conditions to Tarifa—here's how to read it, gear for it, and ride it safely.
body_html:
Understanding These Conditions

The Poniente wind is Tarifa's signature westerly breeze, flowing from the Atlantic across the Strait of Gibraltar. Unlike the more erratic Levante (easterly), the Poniente is remarkably predictable and often the preferred choice for kitesurfers of all levels. It typically arrives in the afternoon, building steadily from 12 to 20+ knots, and can maintain glassy-to-slightly-choppy conditions for hours.

What makes the Poniente wind at Tarifa special is its reliability during summer months and its interaction with the local geography. The wind funnels across the water with clean, well-developed swell from the Atlantic, creating ideal conditions for wave riding and freeriding. You'll notice the wind is strongest between 2 p.m. and sunset, though morning sessions are possible if you arrive early enough.

Water temperature ranges from 15–18°C depending on the season, so layering matters. The Poniente wind at Tarifa doesn't typically create the gusty, thermal-heavy conditions you'd find in enclosed bays—instead, it's a steady, oceanic breeze that rewards good positioning and patience.

Best Gear for These Conditions

For the Poniente at Tarifa, you'll want versatility. When the wind averages 14–16 knots, a 15 m² or 17 m² wave kite performs beautifully; if you're expecting 18+ knots, drop to 12 m² or 14 m². We stock Duotone and Cabrinha kites that excel in these medium-wind, slightly choppy scenarios—the Duotone Rebel stands out for its drift and turning precision in 15–20 knot range.

Board-wise, a directional or hybrid wave board (around 53–58 liters) gives you the control you need for unhooked tricks and wave riding. If swell is mellow, a freestyle-oriented board works too, but the Poniente often brings enough shape that you'll want something designed for direction changes. Check JP Australia and Fanatic offerings—both deliver boards that handle Tarifa's Atlantic swell well.

Wetsuits are non-negotiable. A 5/3 mm hooded suit from ION keeps you warm during longer sessions; in summer, a 4/3 mm or even a 3/2 mm shorty can work, but the Atlantic stays cool year-round. Look at ION's Trinity or Onyx lines for the blend of flexibility and thermal protection Tarifa demands.

Don't overlook your bar and lines. The Poniente wind at Tarifa is steady but not light—a responsive bar (Duotone, Cabrinha) with quality lines prevents fatigue during afternoon sessions when conditions stay strong.

Technique Tips

The Poniente rewards positioning and patience. Because it builds steadily, start your session on the larger side and have a smaller kite ready. Position yourself upwind of the main beach; the wind is strongest and cleanest 200–400 metres out.

Use the swell smartly. Most Poniente days bring 1–2 metre Atlantic swell—work the wave face rather than powering through it. Unhooked manoeuvres are where this wind shines; the consistent pressure lets you load your kite and land tricks with minimal drama. Keep your approach to the wave angled slightly upwind to maximise your power window.

Watch for lulls in the afternoon. Even steady Poniente winds dip briefly; anticipate them and stay moving to maintain leverage. On stronger days (20+ knots), edge hard and use your kite's speed to compensate for the chop.

Safety Checklist

  • Offshore gusts: Even predictable Poniente can gust suddenly. Keep distance from the shoreline and use a kite with good relaunch characteristics (avoid floppy designs).
  • Crowded line-ups: Tarifa is popular. Check conditions and arrive early to claim space, and always maintain 100+ metres from other riders.
  • Leash and rescue: Atlantic swells can shift quickly. Wear a leash, carry a whistle, and know the beach rescue protocols at your spot.
  • Cold water: Even in summer, water temperature stays cool. Wear an appropriate wetsuit and avoid prolonged exposure if you're not accustomed to it.
  • Rig conservatively: When Poniente strengthens unexpectedly, you're better undersized than overpowered. A smaller kite keeps you in control.

Our Gear Recommendations at Surf Store

Duotone Rebel 17 m²: Designed for wave and freestyle, the Rebel excels in 14–18 knot Poniente conditions. Its progressive-curve profile keeps you drifting smoothly even in choppy moments, and relaunch is bulletproof. Available at Surf Store.

ION Onyx Hooded Wetsuit (5/3 mm): Essential for Tarifa's year-round water temps. Stretchy, durable, and warm—this suit won't limit your movement during unhooked tricks. Find it at Surf Store.

JP Australia Wave Board 56L: Balanced rocker, solid pop, and directional drive make this the go-to for Poniente sessions with organised swell. Shop now at Surf Store.

title: Kitesurfing the Bora — Croatia Adriatic Wind Guide
meta_title: Bora Wind Croatia Kitesurfing Guide 2026
meta_description: Master the Bora wind in Croatia with expert kitesurfing and windsurfing tips, gear recommendations, and safety advice. Shop now at Surf Store.
excerpt: The Bora wind transforms Croatia's coast into a world-class kitesurfing and windsurfing destination—learn conditions, gear, technique, and safety.
body_html:
Understanding These Conditions

The Bora wind in Croatia is a northeasterly gale that descends from the Dinaric Alps, channelled down the Adriatic coast with dramatic intensity. This katabatic wind builds momentum as it funnels through mountain passes, arriving at spots like Bol on Brač Island, Novalja on Pag, and the waters around Dalmatia with consistent 15–30 knot gusts—sometimes reaching 40+ knots in winter.

What makes the Bora wind Croatia phenomenon so valuable for water sports is its predictability. Unlike fickle thermal winds, the Bora arrives in defined winter and early-spring windows, typically December through March, with occasional autumn flare-ups. You'll recognise it by clear skies, cold air, and waves that build rapidly as the wind opposes the Adriatic's swell. Wind gusts are strong but relatively smooth once you're away from the immediate shoreline—ideal for serious kitesurfing and windsurfing.

The Bora wind Croatia generates excellent sideshore and slightly onshore conditions depending on your beach orientation. Spots facing northwest enjoy nearly perfect alignment; sheltered bays can be choppy at first, then glassy once the wind fully establishes. Water temperature in peak Bora season hovers around 10–12°C, so thermal protection is non-negotiable.

Best Gear for These Conditions

For kitesurfing in Bora wind Croatia conditions, you'll need a smaller kite than you might expect. A 13–17 m² kite handles the consistent, powerful gusts without overpowering you. The Duotone Evo or Cabrinha FX offer excellent drift and control in gusty, choppy water. Pair these with a stiff freestyle or wave board in the 135–145 cm range—the Nobile North Shore or Fanatic Slice give you the edge hold and pop needed to punch through Bora chop.

Windsurfers should pack a 4.0–4.7 m² sail for the heavy gusts and a 5.0–5.5 m² for moderate days. The NeilPryde Wave or Gaastra Manic deliver the balance of power and forgiveness that gusty conditions demand. Use a narrow, responsive wave board (75–85 L) such as the JP Australia Pro or Tabou Pocket Rocket. The Bora's wind window is wide, so directional boards excel.

Wetsuits are critical. Water temperature demands a 4/3 mm or thicker wetsuit—ION's Onyx or Apex range provides excellent mobility without compromising warmth. Consider bootees, gloves, and a hood; cold air accelerates heat loss, and Bora mornings are brutal. A rashguard layer underneath adds comfort during long sessions.

Technique Tips

The Bora wind's gusts require active weight management. Keep your stance wide and bend your knees; this lowers your centre of gravity and lets you absorb sudden pressure spikes. In kitesurfing, work your bar pressure rather than your body—small, responsive bar movements trim the kite more effectively than shifting your weight dramatically.

Anticipate lulls between gusts. The Bora delivers rhythmic patterns: a gust builds for 10–20 seconds, then briefly eases. Use lulls to edge upwind and reset your position; use gusts to build speed and lock your rail. Windsurfers should aim for early jibes before the next gust hits, keeping you ahead of the wind shift.When the Bora wind Croatia gusts hard, aim for the flatter water further offshore rather than battling chop at the shoreline. Most beaches experience a 50–100 m zone of chaotic whitecap water; beyond that, the wind smooths and the swell lines become clear. Your riding will improve dramatically, and you'll conserve energy.

Safety Checklist

  • Launch and landing: Bora gusts are strongest near the shore. Have a buddy assist your setup, secure loose equipment, and never leave your kite unattended.
  • Hypothermia risk: Water and air temperatures are both cold. Limit sessions to 90 minutes in peak winter; take breaks onshore to warm up and rehydrate with hot drinks.
  • Current awareness: The Bora pushes water offshore, creating rip currents. Stay aware of your position and drift; use landmarks to gauge your set.
  • Visibility: Clear skies mean strong sun glare off the water. Wear polarised sunglasses to protect your eyes and spot hazards.
  • Offshore wind risk: If the Bora strengthens beyond your skill level, exit immediately. There is no shame in calling it—the wind will be back tomorrow.
  • Check conditions daily: The Bora is predictable but not constant. Monitor local wind forecasts and speak to other riders on the beach before committing your time.

Our Gear Recommendations at Surf Store

At Surf Store, we stock everything you need to master the Bora wind in Croatia. For kitesurfing, the Duotone Evo 14 m² is our top pick for consistent, gusty conditions—it offers predictable power delivery and excellent relaunch. Pair it with the Fanatic Slice 140, a wave board that cuts through chop and holds an edge even in confused water.

Windsurfers should consider the NeilPryde Wave 4.5 m² combined with the JP Australia Pro 80—a timeless duo that handles Bora power beautifully. For thermal protection, the ION Onyx 4/3 mm wetsuit is our staff favourite; it balances warmth and mobility across long winter sessions.

Our team has logged countless sessions in Bora conditions across Croatia's coast. We understand the specific demands of this wind, and we're here to help you choose the right kit. Contact us at Surf Store or visit our site to discuss your experience level and session goals—we'll ensure you're properly equipped and confident on the water.

title: The Sirocco Wind — Mediterranean Kitesurfing Guide
meta_title: Sirocco Wind Mediterranean Kitesurfing Guide 2026
meta_description: Master sirocco wind conditions for Mediterranean kitesurfing. Learn gear selection, technique, and safety. Shop now at Surf Store.
excerpt: The sirocco wind brings warm, gusty conditions to Mediterranean waters—here's how to harness it safely and effectively.
body_html:
Understanding These Conditions

The sirocco wind is a hot, dry airflow that sweeps north from the Sahara across the Mediterranean, typically arriving in spring and autumn. What makes sirocco wind Mediterranean kitesurfing unique is the combination of strong thermal gusts, variable direction shifts, and often reduced water visibility due to dust and suspended sediment.

You'll experience sustained winds of 18–28 knots with frequent gusts pushing 35+ knots. The wind rarely flows smoothly; instead, it pulses in waves, creating moments of relative calm followed by sudden acceleration. Water temperatures range from cool (12–15°C in winter) to warm (22–24°C in autumn), but the contrast between air and water can be dramatic, affecting pressure systems and gust intensity.

The sirocco wind Mediterranean kitesurfing scenario typically lasts 2–4 days per weather event. Recognise the signs: a dusty haze on the horizon, rapidly rising temperatures, and pressure drops that make your ears pop. These conditions demand respect and precise preparation.

Best Gear for These Conditions

Kite sizing is critical. In steady 20-knot sirocco wind conditions, most riders go one size smaller than they would in consistent coastal breezes—the gustiness means you're always managing overpowered moments. A 14m kite works well for intermediate riders in average sirocco gusts; beginners should consider a 17m for better lag and forgiveness. For the strongest gusts, drop to 12m or even 10m.

We recommend Duotone Evo or Cabrinha Switchblade for sirocco wind Mediterranean kitesurfing because their delta-quad designs excel at dampening sudden gusts without sacrificing drift and hangtime. Both track reliably when the wind direction shifts 15–20 degrees—a common sirocco characteristic.

Board choice matters enormously. Select a wave or freestyle board in the 42–50 litre range if you're 75–85 kg; smaller boards require more power control but offer agility for chop. A JP Australia Pro or Fanatic Falcon handles chaotic sirocco gusts better than bigger volume freestyle boards because the narrower tail keeps you locked in during acceleration.

Wetsuits: the sirocco wind Mediterranean kitesurfing scenario demands thermal protection. In spring and autumn, pair a 3/2mm springsuit with an ION Versatility Jacket for extra torso warmth. In winter, upgrade to 4/3mm full chest zip—the air temperature can feel cold despite the sirocco's desert origin, and water immersion time adds up. Gloves are essential; neoprene or semi-dry options prevent fatigue and maintain bar control during long sessions.

Technique Tips

Adjust your stance for instability. Keep your feet wider apart than usual, centring your weight over the board's sweet spot. When a gust hits, stay quiet with your bar hand—resist the impulse to edge hard. Instead, allow the board to drift slightly under you while maintaining heel pressure. This absorbs energy and prevents catapults.

Work your kite deliberately in the sirocco wind Mediterranean kitesurfing context. Keep your kite at 45 degrees or lower in the wind window during gusts; this reduces lift and shortens recovery time if you lose power in a lull. Use small, controlled loops for tricks rather than powered launches. The wind's unpredictability punishes aggressive styling.

During lulls—common in sirocco events—immediately return your kite to 12 o'clock and lean back. Foot pressure matters more than bar tension. When the next gust arrives, you'll be ready to sheet in and control the acceleration rather than being caught off-guard.

Safety Checklist

  • Visibility: Sirocco dust reduces sight lines. Use bright kite colours (lime, hot pink) and wear a high-visibility rashguard. Stay within shouting distance of other riders.
  • Dehydration: The dry, hot air accelerates fluid loss. Drink water before, during (via a hydration pack), and after sessions. Avoid alcohol the night before.
  • Gust management: Never sail alone in sirocco conditions. Use a land spotter. Check your safety release before every session.
  • Equipment inspection: Inspect bridles, leading edges, and bar tension. Damaged gear becomes dangerous under gust stress.
  • Launch and landing: Launch from a beach wide enough to avoid crowding; kites can drift unpredictably. Have an assistant or use a solid anchor point.

Our Gear Recommendations at Surf Store

For mastering sirocco wind Mediterranean kitesurfing, we stock NeilPryde Dice kites in 12m and 14m sizes—excellent all-around performers for variable conditions. Visit surf-store.com to compare.

Pair your kite with an ION Onyx Wetsuit (3/2mm) for spring and autumn sessions. Its thermal lining keeps core temperature stable while the stretch neoprene ensures mobility. Check our wetsuits collection.

Finally, if you're upgrading your board, the Tabou Roam or Nobile 2023 Wave both handle choppy, gusty sirocco conditions with aplomb. Our expert staff at Surf Store can advise you on the best size for your weight and skill level.

title: Trade Wind Kitesurfing — Why It Is So Good
meta_title: Trade Winds Kitesurfing Canary Islands 2026 | Fuerteventu...
meta_description: Master trade winds kitesurfing in Fuerteventura. Learn conditions, gear sizes, technique tips & safety. Shop kites & boards at Surf Store.
excerpt: Trade winds deliver reliable, consistent Atlantic conditions for kitesurfing in Fuerteventura. Learn how to read them, choose the right gear, and ride safely.
body_html:
Understanding These Conditions

Trade winds are the Atlantic's gift to Fuerteventura and the Canary Islands. These northeast-to-east winds blow consistently year-round, shaped by the Azores high-pressure system. Unlike thermal or land-sea breeze patterns that peter out by afternoon, trade winds maintain steady 12–20 knots throughout the day, often building into the afternoon. In winter (November–March), gusts can exceed 25 knots, while summer sees lighter, steadier 10–15 knot sessions.

What makes trade winds ideal for trade winds kitesurfing Canary Islands enthusiasts is their predictability. You'll find them blowing offshore or cross-shore at most spots around Fuerteventura, creating clean wave organisation and minimal gustiness. The wind tends to arrive early morning, peak by mid-afternoon, and drop slightly after sunset. This rhythm lets you plan your sessions with confidence—no chasing thermal pockets or waiting for sea breezes to build.

The trade winds also interact with Fuerteventura's geography. East-facing beaches receive direct offshore winds, while northern and southern shores catch cross-shore or sideshore conditions. Understanding this geography is crucial: a spot that's marginal in true trade wind season might be excellent during autumn transitions when the wind swings more easterly.

Best Gear for These Conditions

Trade winds demand kites and boards matched to consistent, moderate power delivery. For trade winds kitesurfing in Fuerteventura's typical 12–20 knot range, a 14–17 m² wave kite is your workhorse. The Duotone Rebel or Cabrinha Switchblade excel here: they generate enough drive in lighter gusts yet don't overpower in 20-knot peaks. In winter storms (25+ knots), drop to 12 m² or swap to a freestyle kite if the bay flattens.

Board selection matters equally. Wave boards (85–95 litres, 55–65 cm wide) are the go-to choice for Fuerteventura's reef and beach breaks. A JP Australia Pro or Fanatic Wave will turn crisply in chop and release power efficiently. If you're riding sideshore, a directional freestyle board (90–100 litres) lets you experiment with tricks without fighting the wind's constant push.

Wetsuits are often overlooked in sub-tropical trade wind zones. Water temperatures in Fuerteventura stay 16–20 °C year-round, so a 3/2 mm steamer is essential—ION Thermal or NeilPryde Thermaxx keep you warm during long winter sessions without overheating in summer. Avoid oversized suits; snug fits reduce bulk and let you move freely in smaller boards.

Don't forget a solid bar-and-line setup. Point7 or Gaastra control bars offer smooth steering and reliable safety leash engagement, vital when trade winds gust unexpectedly. A backup spreader bar and lines should always be in your quiver.

Technique Tips

Trade winds reward patience and smooth movements. Because the wind is consistent, edge changes and kite movements are predictable—overcommitting to a manoeuvre often spells disaster. Keep your kite movements controlled and deliberate; small kite adjustments yield big power swings.

In lighter trade wind days (10–15 knots), spend time on edge and steering with your feet rather than yanking the bar. Build rhythm with the swell rather than fighting it. Your pop timing becomes critical: trade winds don't reward late jumps, so concentrate on explosive rail-to-rail transitions.

When gusts arrive (afternoon intensification), angle your kite slightly lower in the window to regulate power. Learn to feather your bar: small pulls and releases prevent arm fatigue and let you float through lulls. Practice body positioning: centred stance with bent knees absorbs chop and keeps you stable through power surges.

Downwind drifts are inevitable in consistent offshore trade winds. Plan your sessions to use the wind's direction: enter from the upwind side and expect to work downwind. Spot your exit before you launch.

Safety Checklist

Predictable doesn't mean risk-free. Trade winds can surprise:

  • Afternoon gusts: Wind strengthens 2–4 knots between noon and 4 p.m. Downsize your kite or quit early if gusts feel uncontrollable.
  • Offshore consistency: Once you're downwind, there's no onshore buffer. Always ride with a safety boat or buddy in visual contact. A drift can carry you far from shore quickly.
  • Gear failure: Inspect bridles, lines, and bar mechanisms before each session. Knots tighten under constant strain; renew them weekly.
  • Fatigue: Consistent wind means long sessions are tempting. Respect your limits; cumulative arm and core fatigue reduces reaction times.
  • Local hazards: Reef breaks, sea-grass beds, and rocky outcrops are common in Fuerteventura. Scout spots on calm days and ask locals about submerged obstacles.

Our Gear Recommendations at Surf Store

For reliable trade winds kitesurfing sessions at Fuerteventura's consistent breaks, we recommend:

  • Duotone Rebel 14 m²: A proven wave kite that balances drive and forgiveness. Handles 12–22 knot trade winds without fuss. Available now at Surf Store.
  • JP Australia Pro Wave 90 L: Quick turning, predictable in gusty trade winds, and tough enough for reef breaks. Pairs perfectly with consistent northeast blows. Shop at Surf Store.
  • ION Thermal 3/2 Steamer: Stays warm in Fuerteventura's winter swells without restricting movement. Essential for year-round comfort. Find it at Surf Store.

title: Sea Breeze & Thermal Wind — Kitesurfing Guide
meta_title: Thermal Wind Sea Breeze Kitesurfing Guide 2026
meta_description: Master thermal sea breeze conditions for kitesurfing. Learn wind patterns, ideal gear, and technique tips. Shop expert gear at Surf Store.
excerpt: Thermal sea breeze creates predictable afternoon wind for kitesurfing—perfect for progression and light-wind sessions when you know how to read it.
body_html:
Understanding These Conditions

A thermal sea breeze is one of the most reliable wind patterns you'll encounter in coastal water sports. It forms when the sun heats the land faster than the sea, creating a pressure difference that pulls cooler air from the water inland. For kitesurfing, this means a steady, predictable wind that typically builds through the morning and peaks in early afternoon—usually between 12:00 and 16:00. The thermal wind sea breeze rarely exceeds 18 knots in temperate European locations, making it ideal for learners and light-wind specialists, but it's also surprisingly consistent once established.

The wind usually comes perpendicular or nearly parallel to the coast, depending on local geography. You'll notice it strengthens as the day heats up and dies away as soon as the sun loses power in late afternoon. Unlike squall systems, thermal sea breeze conditions offer excellent visibility and steady pressure, which means fewer surprises and more time to focus on technique.

Best Gear for These Conditions

In a thermal wind sea breeze scenario, you're looking at 10–16 knots for most of the session. This calls for larger kite sizes than strong-wind days: expect to use 15–17 m² kites if you're a heavier rider, or 13–15 m² if you're lighter or more experienced. A wave or freestyle kite like the Duotone Rebel or Cabrinha FX will give you the responsiveness you need while you're working on tricks or cruising in marginal pressure.

Board-wise, choose a directional wave board (around 55–65 litres) or a freestyle board if you're practising unhooked passes. Avoid twintips unless you're comfortable generating your own pop—thermal conditions rarely deliver the grunt needed for big air.

For wetsuits, a 3/2 mm or 4/3 mm ION or Nobile suit is sensible. Thermal days often arrive when water is cool but not freezing, and you'll be spending hours out there. A quality wetsuit keeps you comfortable so you can focus on progression rather than shivering between runs.

Check the NeilPryde or Point7 ranges at Surf Store for kites that excel in light-to-moderate thermals; both brands excel in responsive, playful designs that reward technique over raw power.

Technique Tips

Thermal conditions are forgiving but require respect for wind management. Keep your kite in the power zone more than you would in strong wind—let it do the work. Work your bar movements slowly and deliberately; small inputs translate to big results when the wind is light. If you're learning tricks, thermal sea breeze sessions are perfect for building muscle memory without the adrenaline overload of stronger days.Stay alert to wind direction shifts. A thermal breeze often rotates slightly as the day progresses, and local thermal updrafts can create brief pockets of stronger or lighter wind. Anticipate these by reading the water texture ahead and adjusting your kite position early.

Use longer, smoother water crossings to build rhythm and confidence. Thermal days reward patience: you're less likely to get caught in unexpected gusts, so you can experiment with edge control, carving, and balance without fear.

Safety Checklist

  • Leash and impact vest: Even in light wind, wear both. Thermal sessions often last hours, and fatigue can lead to mistakes.
  • Know your exit window: Thermal wind dies fast after 16:00. Plan to be back on the beach before power drops—a tiring paddle home in zero wind is no fun.
  • Watch for thermals and rotations: Wind can shift 20–30 degrees as the day heats up. Position yourself where you can easily transition if direction changes.
  • Check water temperature: Thermal days arrive in spring and autumn when water is cool. Hypothermia risk is real on 3–4 hour sessions—don't skip the wetsuit.
  • Communicate with other riders: Thermal spots attract crowds. Agree on zones and watch for entanglement.

Our Gear Recommendations at Surf Store

For thermal wind sea breeze sessions, we recommend the Duotone Rebel 15 m²—a versatile, responsive kite that shines in 10–15 knot conditions and forgives imprecise bar movements while you're learning. It's ideal for both wave riding and light freestyle.

Pair it with an ION Wetsuit 4/3 mm in neoprene for comfort on longer sessions, especially in spring and autumn when thermal breeze conditions peak. You'll stay warm, stay focused, and get more value from every session.

Finally, grab a JP Australia wave board 60 L for directional control and progressive technique building. Browse our full range of kites, boards, and wetsuits at Surf Store—our team rides these conditions year-round and can help you dial in the perfect setup for your level.

title: Kitesurfing in 20 Knots — Kite Size & Setup Guide
meta_title: Kitesurfing 20 Knots: What Kite Size 2026
meta_description: 20 knots is ideal for progression. Learn kite sizes, techniques & safety for kitesurfing in 20 knots. Find gear at Surf Store.
excerpt: 20 knots offers perfect conditions for learning and progression—here's how to choose your kite size and master the wind.
body_html:
Understanding These Conditions

20 knots sits in that sweet spot where kitesurfing transitions from manageable for beginners into seriously fun riding. It's steady enough that you won't be fighting unstable gusts, yet powerful enough to deliver genuine lift and speed. European spots like Neusiedl Lake in Austria or the North Sea often see consistent 20-knot days, particularly in spring and autumn.

At this wind speed, you're dealing with moderate energy in the water. The chop is typically manageable, kites respond predictably, and mistakes won't leave you stranded. For riders progressing from lighter winds or stepping down from heavier conditions, kitesurfing 20 knots rewards clean technique without punishing you for imperfect execution. Most serious riders consider this the ideal practice wind.

Best Gear for These Conditions

The critical question for kitesurfing 20 knots: what kite size should you fly?

Kite Size: For an average adult (75–85 kg), a 14m or 15m kite is your baseline. Heavier riders or those wanting more pop should lean toward 15m or 17m; lighter athletes (under 70 kg) can work comfortably on 12m or 14m. The Duotone Evo and Cabrinha Switchblade are excellent mid-range performers in this category—responsive, stable, and forgiving enough to build consistency.

Board Selection: Choose a freestyle or wave board in the 135–145 cm range. Your board needs enough volume to ride upwind in moderate pressure without bogging down. A JP Australia Pro or Fanatic Quad Quad gives you versatility for both cruising and tricks.

Wetsuit: Depending on your location's water temperature, a 3/2 mm or 4/3 mm wetsuit keeps you warm during extended sessions. ION winter suits offer superior stretch and durability; in warmer months, a 2/2 mm spring suit works well. Pair it with ION or NeilPryde booties to maintain grip and warmth.

Technique Tips

At 20 knots, focus on generating speed through efficient edge control rather than relying on raw wind power. Your kite work becomes smoother—make smaller, deliberate movements rather than aggressive slashing. This is the perfect wind to drill your up-wind angle: maintain control by keeping your shoulders square to your board and letting the kite's power drive you, not your arms.

For powered riding, the wind gives you enough energy to practice advanced manoeuvres like pop tricks or wave transitions. Conversely, if conditions drop toward 18 knots, you'll need that extra body awareness to maintain momentum. Keep your stance centred and your weight over your board's sweet spot.

Use this wind to build muscle memory. 20 knots demands consistency but forgives hesitation—perfect for cementing fundamentals before you chase stronger conditions or attempt radical freestyle.

Safety Checklist

  • Pre-Flight Inspection: Check bridle lines, bladder integrity, and spreader bars. Even at moderate wind, a failing bladder can collapse your kite unexpectedly.
  • Spot Selection: Confirm your launch zone is clear of obstacles and spectators. At 20 knots, you'll drift significantly; ensure your downwind margin is generous.
  • Buddy System: Always kitesurf with another rider or spotter visible. 20 knots won't trap you, but unexpected gusts can surprise you.
  • Leash & Safety Release: Wear your helmet, leash your board, and keep your safety release immediately accessible. Know how your particular kite's quick-release mechanism works.
  • Tidal Awareness: In coastal zones, check tides before launching. A 20-knot session in changing tide can drift you quickly along the shoreline.
  • Weather Monitoring: Watch for wind shifts or squalls. Afternoon heating can spike gusts; early morning is often steadier.

Our Gear Recommendations at Surf Store

For your next 20-knot session, we stock everything you need to progress with confidence. The Duotone Evo 15m is our go-to recommendation for this wind range—it delivers predictable power delivery and sits beautifully in drifts. Pair it with a Fanatic Quad Quad 140cm board for a versatile all-rounder that excels at moderate wind speeds. Complete your kit with a ION Onyx 4/3 mm wetsuit if you're riding in cooler European waters. All three products are available at surf-store.com with expert sizing guidance—our staff has logged hundreds of hours in exactly these conditions and can help you dial in your personal setup.

title: Kitesurfing in 25–30 Knots — Strong Wind Setup Guide
meta_title: Kitesurfing 25–30 Knots: Guide for Strong Wind 2026
meta_description: Master strong-wind kitesurfing in 25–30 knots. Gear, technique, and safety tips from Surf Store experts. Shop now at Surf Store.
excerpt: 25–30 knots is prime strong-wind territory for experienced kiters. Learn the right gear, technique, and safety measures to ride these conditions confidently.
body_html:
Understanding These Conditions

Kitesurfing in 25–30 knots sits firmly in the strong-wind category—this is where things get exciting and demanding. At this wind speed, you're looking at chop, genuine power delivery, and genuine consequences for mistakes. The conditions are neither casual nor extreme; they're the sweet spot where experienced riders find the most technical challenge and the best pop.

Wind at 25–30 knots delivers sustained pressure that makes your kite sit high and responsive. Water state will be choppy to slightly rough depending on fetch and water depth. Gusts will push above your average, so you need a board and kite combination that keeps you in control rather than overpowered. These conditions are ideal for progression riders and intermediate-to-advanced kiters who've logged solid hours in 15–20 knot winds.

Best Gear for These Conditions

Your kite size is critical when kitesurfing 25 30 knots. Most riders will drop down to a 12 or 13 m² kite—possibly smaller if you're heavier or chasing raw speed. A 14 m² might still work if you're light and the wind sits at the lower end of the range, but be honest about your control. Brands like Duotone and Cabrinha design modern freeride and wave kites that are forgiving in gusts and progressive under load.

Your board should be relatively small and manoeuvrable. A 36–41 litre directional or wave-focused board keeps you responsive and lets you edge hard without spinning out. Nobile and JP Australia make excellent boards for this spectrum. Avoid large freestyle boards—you'll struggle to control them in chop, and they'll catch too much wind.

Wetsuit choice matters too. A 3/2 mm spring suit from ION works for summer sessions, but spring and autumn demand a 4/3 mm or 5/3 mm. Cold water will sap your focus and reaction time, so invest in proper insulation. A hood and gloves become non-negotiable as air temperature drops.

Technique Tips

In 25–30 knot kitesurfing conditions, your stance and edge control separate clean rides from wash-outs. Keep your weight back and your knees bent—this absorbs chop and lets you absorb gusts without being yanked upwind. Ride slightly toe-edge on your heels to maintain pressure and prevent accidental upwind drift.

Your kite work must be precise. Avoid aggressive figure-eights; instead, make smaller, controlled movements to fine-tune power. Work your bar pressure—ease the bar slightly into gusts rather than steering hard, which only increases load. Practice riding switch (backwards) for a few minutes each session; it builds board awareness and keeps you calm under pressure.

Pop and tricks demand explosive timing. Wait for the kite to be directly overhead before sending your jump; diving the kite in gusty conditions can cause overrotation and hard crashes. Similarly, waits for calm moments between gusts to attempt freestyle tricks—chop and wind variation will sabotage your edge control.

Safety Checklist

  • Helmet and impact vest are non-negotiable. Chop creates harder crashes, and your reaction time shrinks under adrenaline. Protect your head and ribs.
  • Check your equipment before launch. Inspect your bar, lines, and bridle for damage. A line break at 25–30 knots will cost you speed and control fast.
  • Launch with a buddy. Never session strong wind alone. Someone on shore can spot hazards and call for help if needed.
  • Know your exit strategy. Identify a clear downwind zone where you can safely release your kite and swim to shore. Avoid rocky breaks and busy shipping lanes.
  • Avoid relaunching in gusts. If you fall, wait for wind to settle before attempting a relaunch. A botched relaunch in 28 knots can yank you across the water or into obstacles.
  • Watch the sky. Squalls and sudden wind shifts happen. If the sky darkens or wind direction swings, head in immediately.

Our Gear Recommendations at Surf Store

For kitesurfing 25–30 knots, we stock purpose-built kit from Europe's trusted brands. The Duotone Rebel 12 m² is a freeride workhorse that stays stable in gusts and hooks well in chop. Pair it with a Nobile NHP 38L wave board for responsive edge control and snappy turns. For protection, the ION Thermo 5/3 mm Zip wetsuit keeps you warm and mobile even in colder water.

Visit surf-store.com to compare kite sizes, board widths, and wetsuit thickness. Our team rides these conditions regularly and can advise on the right setup for your weight, skill level, and local water state. We ship across Europe and offer expert guidance before you commit to new gear.

title: Kitesurfing in 12–15 Knots — Light Wind Tips & Gear
meta_title: Kitesurfing Light Wind 12–15 Knots: Tips & Gear 2026
meta_description: Master kitesurfing light wind 12–15 knots with our expert guide on kite sizes, technique, and safety. Find gear at Surf Store.
excerpt: Learn how to kitesurfing light wind 12–15 knots conditions with the right kite size, technique adjustments, and safety practices.
body_html:
Understanding These Conditions

Kitesurfing light wind 12–15 knots is where patience meets precision. At this wind strength, you're right at the threshold where many riders struggle to get going, but experienced kitesurfers know these sessions offer incredible learning opportunities and smooth, predictable riding. The wind sits between marginal and workable—enough to generate power, but only if your setup and technique are dialled in perfectly.

These light conditions typically occur during early mornings, late afternoons, or during summer doldrums when thermal winds dominate. Water tends to be glassy and flat, which means minimal chop to hide your mistakes, but also maximum control and edge feel. If you're chasing consistent power in 12–15 knots, you'll need oversized equipment and flawless technique to maintain momentum and avoid floating dead in the water.

Best Gear for These Conditions

When kitesurfing light wind 12–15 knots, your kite size becomes critical. Most riders need to go 2–3 sizes larger than their normal quiver. If you usually ride a 14 m², you're looking at a 17 m² or even 19 m² for reliable performance in these marginal winds. Duotone's Evo or NeilPryde's Wave kites excel in light wind because their refined bridle geometry maintains shape and power even when apparent wind is low.

For your board, favour lighter, wider, and more buoyant designs. A 51–55-litre wave or freeride board gives you flotation without sacrificing control. Many riders find success with directional shapes—JP Australia's Pro series or Fanatic's Falcon offer forgiving platforms that catch waves and hold power even at minimal speeds.

Your wetsuit matters more than you'd think. ION's 3/2 mm suits keep you warm during extended sessions without adding bulk that restricts movement. Thinner chest panels improve paddling comfort, which you'll be doing more of if you lose the wind mid-session.

Technique Tips

Kitesurfing light wind 12–15 knots demands active kite work. Keep your kite moving constantly—figure-eights and pendulum motions in the window generate apparent wind and keep tension on the bar. Never park your kite at 12 o'clock; it dies there. Work it between 11 and 1 o'clock, using snappy movements to build power when you need it.

Adopt a wider, more relaxed stance than normal. This lowers your centre of gravity and distributes your weight better across a less responsive board. Keep your shoulders forward and your hips engaged—let the board do the work rather than muscling it. When cruising, maintain consistent speed by chaining smooth turns together; stopping to regroup wastes what little power you've built.

Body drag is your friend in these conditions. Use it to reset position without losing momentum, and practise launching from the water without assistance—you might need to do it multiple times in a single session if the wind drops unpredictably.

Safety Checklist

  • Wind Window Awareness: Light wind means less margin for error. Know exactly where your dead zone is and avoid drifting there, especially over obstacles or away from your exit point.
  • Rescue Fitness: Have a solid self-rescue plan and the fitness to execute it. A tow-in might not be available, and you may need to swim or paddle back to shore.
  • Spot Selection: Choose a beach with consistent sideshore wind and clear ground hazards. Light wind makes you vulnerable to wind shifts and thermal effects—a shaded beach might have 2 knots less than the sunny section 200 metres away.
  • Communication: Tell someone on shore when you're heading out and when you expect to return. Light wind sessions can get extended or cut short unpredictably.
  • Kite Inspection: Check bridles, lines, and bladders carefully. Oversized kites in marginal wind put extra strain on components.

Our Gear Recommendations at Surf Store

For kitesurfing light wind 12–15 knots, we stock the Duotone Evo 17 m², a purpose-built light-wind weapon that maintains shape and drive even when the breeze drops. Its refined bridle geometry and responsive handling make it perfect for thermal sessions and early-morning missions across Europe.

Pair it with the JP Australia Pro Wave 51L, a forgiving yet high-performance board that rewards smooth technique and stays underfoot even when power is marginal. The extra volume gives you flotation without sacrificing rail sharpness or responsiveness.

Complete your setup with ION's Neoprene 3/2 mm suit for comfort during long sessions when you're generating your own heat through constant effort. Check out our full kitesurfing range at surf-store.com for sizing advice and current stock across all these conditions.

title: Windsurfing in 20 Knots — Sail Size & Setup Guide
meta_title: Windsurfing 20 Knots Sail Size Setup 2026 | Surf Store
meta_description: Expert guide to windsurfing in 20 knots: ideal sail sizes, boards, and technique. Find your perfect setup at Surf Store.
excerpt: 20 knots is ideal windsurfing conditions. Learn the right sail size, board choice, and technique to make the most of moderate wind.
body_html:
Understanding These Conditions

20 knots is the sweet spot for windsurfing—consistent, predictable, and fun for riders of most skill levels. At this wind strength, you've got enough power to plane easily without fighting excessive gusts, and the water conditions tend to be manageable with small, organised chop rather than chaotic swell. This is when windsurfing feels effortless: you're generating real speed, your board sits flat, and you can focus on style and control rather than survival.

In a windsurfing 20 knots sail size setup, the wind typically comes from a consistent direction with minimal variation. Thermals and sea breezes often stabilize around this strength in early morning or late afternoon sessions across Europe. If you're in the Mediterranean or Baltic, 20 knots often arrives as a predictable afternoon wind, giving you a clean window to get on the water.

Best Gear for These Conditions

For windsurfing 20 knots, your sail size is crucial. Intermediate to advanced riders should reach for a 4.0–4.5 m² sail; beginners might prefer 4.5–5.0 m² to maintain confidence and control. A 4.2 m² is the true middle ground and works brilliantly across a range of conditions just above and below 20 knots.

At Surf Store, we stock Duotone, NeilPryde, and Gaastra sails—all designed for real-world European conditions. The Duotone Viper is a popular choice for these conditions: it accelerates quickly, handles gusts without sudden luff, and offers excellent manoeuvrability. The NeilPryde Combat delivers a similar feel with slightly more forgiving handling for intermediate sailors.

Your board should sit between 75 and 90 litres if you're an intermediate rider. A wave or freeride board (not a pure slalom board) gives you forgiveness, easier turning, and better control in choppy water. JP Australia and Fanatic both produce boards that excel in these conditions and are stocked at Surf Store.

For wetsuits, a 3/2 mm thickness is standard in spring and autumn across Europe; 4/3 mm or 5/4 mm in winter. ION wetsuits at Surf Store combine premium neoprene stretch with reliable durability—critical when you're working hard in moderate wind.

Technique Tips

In 20 knots, focus on clean footwork and efficient sail trim. Your boom height should sit roughly at rib-cage level; too low and you'll block your vision and limit upwind ability.

  • Harness technique: Lean back slightly more than in light wind, but don't over-commit. Your weight should stay centred on the board so you can react to gusts without a sudden catapult.
  • Boom pressure: Keep your back hand light—let the sail power itself rather than muscling it. This reduces arm fatigue and improves control.
  • Foot position: In 20 knots, your feet should be slightly further forward than in stronger wind. This helps the board respond quicker and keeps you from stalling in lulls.
  • Gust management: When a gust hits, ease the boom forward slightly and twist your hips upwind. Don't sheet in harder—let the sail's design handle the extra power.

Safety Checklist

20 knots is forgiving but not risk-free. Before launching:

  • Check your rig: Loose battens or a worn mast can collapse unexpectedly in gusts. Test your boom clamp, mast foot, and all hardware.
  • Wear a leash: Even experienced sailors use a leash in moderate wind. Board separation is both dangerous and embarrassing.
  • Assess the water: Look for currents, shallow patches, or shipping lanes. 20 knots gives you real speed; you'll cover distance quickly.
  • Have a plan to exit: Know where you'll land. Offshore wind can push you away from the beach; onshore wind can make return entry tough if the shore is crowded or rocky.
  • Watch the forecast: 20 knots often sits between two systems. Check if conditions are strengthening or dropping, and plan your session length accordingly.

Our Gear Recommendations at Surf Store

Building the ideal windsurfing 20 knots sail size setup starts with the right sail. The Duotone Viper 4.2 m² is our top pick for these conditions: it generates power smoothly, accelerates without drama, and handles gusts with maturity. Pair it with a JP Australia Wave 85 board for a forgiving, fun combination that rewards technique and builds confidence.

Footwear and protection matter too. Our ION 3/2 mm Wetsuit (available in men's and women's cuts) keeps you warm and mobile, crucial for longer sessions when you're maximising these reliable conditions. Shop these essentials and more at Surf Store today.

title: Windsurfing in 30 Knots — Strong Wind Tips & Gear
meta_title: Windsurfing Strong Wind 30 Knots Tips 2026
meta_description: Master windsurfing in strong wind with expert 30-knot guides, gear picks, and technique tips. Shop quality sails and boards at Surf Store.
excerpt: 30-knot conditions demand the right sail size, board control, and safety awareness—here's how to read the conditions and thrive.
body_html:
Understanding These Conditions

Windsurfing strong wind at 30 knots sits firmly in the intermediate-to-advanced range. This is where real speed happens, where your board lifts across chop, and where technique separates confidence from chaos. At this wind strength, you're experiencing consistent power delivery with waves building texture; chop becomes pronounced, and water dynamics shift dramatically from light-wind sessions.

Thirty knots of wind creates the conditions where hydroplaning begins in earnest—your board will want to lift and skip across the surface rather than cut through it. Your sail fills with genuine load, your harness takes meaningful strain, and your edges become your steering. This is the sweet spot where many windsurfers find their flow, but it also demands respect for wind and water behaviour. Gusts can swing another 5–10 knots either side, so understanding the gust window and your gear's tolerance is essential.

Best Gear for These Conditions

For windsurfing strong wind at 30 knots, sail size is your primary control variable. Look for sails in the 3.5–4.5 m² range, depending on your weight, skill, and board choice. Lighter riders may comfortably handle 4.5 m²; heavier sailors often prefer stepping down to 3.8 m² for better control and response.

We recommend Duotone's Rebel sails—built specifically for high-wind performance with a batten profile that maintains shape under load and a leech design that sheds gusts naturally. The Rebel's wave-orientated cut suits 30-knot chop brilliantly. Alternatively, NeilPryde's Fusion sails offer excellent upwind drive and gust tolerance; many of our customers find them forgiving when conditions spike.

Your board should be 75–95 litres—responsive, narrow in the tail, and edgy. JP Australia's Wave boards excel here; their rocker profile and rail geometry let you carve aggressively while maintaining control in choppy water. Fanatic's Freewave range also delivers superb handling for this wind range, with enough volume to keep you floating during lulls.

Don't skimp on your wetsuit. ION's 4/3 Aventail jackets provide warmth, mobility, and durability when you're working hard in 30 knots—your body temperature rises during intense sessions, but water chill is real. A high-quality suit keeps you focused on technique rather than shivering.

Technique Tips

In windsurfing strong wind situations, your stance changes. Lower your harness hook slightly; engage your legs more, relying less on arm strength. The water is moving faster, so your weight distribution matters more—stay centred, with your front foot driving you forward and your back foot controlling the tail. In 30 knots, the board wants to escape; you must command it.

Keep your boom height consistent. Raising it slightly reduces pressure in gusts; lowering it increases drive in lulls. Your hands should be relaxed yet alert—death grip on the boom tires you quickly and kills responsiveness. Let the harness carry the load; your hands steer and adjust.

Work your edges early. Initiate turns by dropping an edge and rotating your hips before committing your shoulders. This prevents the board spinning uncontrollably and keeps you in control during strong wind transitions. On the wave face, use heel-side pressure to lock into turns; rail pressure is your brake and your propellant.

Practice your exits. In 30 knots, a dismount gone wrong can leave you swimming. Always release the boom cleanly, lean back, and step off the board towards its tail. Never jump forward; never try to bail hard—let gravity and buoyancy do the work.

Safety Checklist

  • Wear a helmet and impact vest. Wave impact at 30 knots demands head protection; an impact vest absorbs tumble energy.
  • Check your gear before launch. Batten tension, boom clamp security, mast foot—all take strain in strong wind. A loose component becomes dangerous quickly.
  • Never sail alone. Have a buddy ashore or on water. Exhaustion sets in faster; rescue becomes essential if you're separated from your board.
  • Know your exit route. Identify how you'll return to shore. In 30 knots, drift is real; plan your approach early.
  • Stay hydrated. Physical exertion in strong wind is taxing. Drink water between sessions or take energy breaks ashore.
  • Respect the forecast. If 30 knots is your limit, don't sail when 40-knot gusts are predicted. The upside risk isn't worth the downside cost.

Our Gear Recommendations at Surf Store

At Surf Store, we stock everything you need for confident windsurfing strong wind performance. Browse our complete windsurfing sails range for Duotone, NeilPryde, and Gaastra options sized perfectly for 30 knots. Our wave and freestyle boards from JP Australia and Fanatic come with expert guidance; tell us your weight and skill level, and we'll recommend the ideal volume and rocker profile. Finally, our ION and Gaastra wetsuit collection ensures you stay warm and mobile. Contact our team—we've sailed these conditions ourselves and love helping you do the same.

title: Windsurfing in Light Wind — Can You Plane in 10–12 Knots?
meta_title: Windsurfing Light Wind 10-12 Knots: Planing Guide 2026
meta_description: Master windsurfing light wind conditions (10–12 knots). Learn sail sizes, board selection, and technique to plane consistently. Find gear at Surf Store.
excerpt: Discover how to plane in light wind and 10–12 knots with the right sail size, board setup, and technique adjustments for consistent performance.
body_html:
Understanding These Conditions

Windsurfing light wind conditions—typically 10–12 knots—sit right at the threshold where planing becomes possible but demands precision. At this wind strength, you're at the edge of your equipment's capability; lightweight riders may plane earlier, whilst heavier sailors need optimal technique and larger sails to trigger lift-off. Light wind also means less margin for error: every movement counts, weight distribution matters enormously, and momentum is your ally. Weather is usually stable, making these ideal learning windows for intermediate riders wanting to refine their planing skills without being knocked around by gusts.

Best Gear for These Conditions

For windsurfing light wind planing at 10–12 knots, sail size is the critical variable. Most riders need 5.0–5.8 m² sails depending on body weight and board volume. Lighter sailors (under 75 kg) can work with a 5.0 m²; heavier or taller riders benefit from 5.5–5.8 m². Look for high-clew designs that provide more leverage lower down and drive through light air. Duotone and NeilPryde sails excel in this range, offering responsive masts and battens that reward correct technique.

Board choice is equally important. Select a freeride or high-volume wave board (85–100 litres for lighter riders, 100–120 litres for heavier sailors) with adequate float to support you before the wind builds. Wider boards carry speed better in light wind; narrower designs sacrifice early-wind performance but plane sooner once conditions improve. JP Australia and Fanatic produce excellent light-wind boards that balance volume with responsiveness.

Footstrap placement and mast positioning influence light-wind planing dramatically. Consider a slightly forward mast foot setting (1–2 cm ahead of your normal position) to free the tail and encourage early lift. Rigger your sail with slightly downward boom angle—this transfers power into the board rather than lifting your body.

Wetsuits protect you during extended light-wind sessions; many occur in cooler mornings or shoulder seasons. ION Seek or ION Onyx 3/2 mm suits provide warmth and mobility without restricting your movement during technical manoeuvres.

Technique Tips

Planing in light wind demands smooth, committed weight transfer. Here's what works: start by sailing lower and building speed before attempting to turn across the wind. Approach your bearing with rail pressure and marginal sail angle—anticipate the gust slightly before it arrives. As you feel power building, shift your weight aft gradually; sudden movements stall the board and waste precious momentum.

Keep your boom height consistent. In light wind, sheeting in too far (closing your sail angle) creates excessive heel without increased drive; instead, maintain a neutral stance and let subtle harness pressure carry the load. Your legs, not your arms, control the board—engage your thighs and core to hold rail and direction.

Use the wave face or chop intelligently. Small ripples carry momentum; carve up the back side of bumps to maintain speed. Avoid dead flat water; seek the slightly rougher patches where wind texturing suggests stronger air.

When sailing upwind, reduce gybing frequency. Each manoeuvre loses speed; in light wind, longer tacks preserve planing velocity. Edge your sail more aggressively during upwind phases and accept slightly wider angles if it keeps you moving.

Safety Checklist

  • Respect the off-shore risk: Light wind can mask off-shore flow. Always check weather patterns before launching; use landmarks on shore to verify true wind direction.
  • Carry a whistle and ID: Light wind means slower speeds and potentially longer drift times if conditions turn. A whistle and contact details improve visibility to rescue services.
  • Use a shorter boom: Lighter wind tempts riders to extend booms for leverage. A 1–2 cm shorter boom improves control and reduces fatigue during technical sessions.
  • Inspect your boom joint: Extended light-wind sessions increase vibration and micro-movement. Check your boom connection and mast-base collar before launching.
  • Stay alert to wind shadows: Headlands, trees, and buildings create unpredictable dead zones. Anticipate where gusts might drop and position yourself accordingly.

Our Gear Recommendations at Surf Store

For consistent light-wind planing, we recommend the Duotone Evo sail range in 5.0–5.8 m²; these sails reward light-wind technique and remain forgiving even if your weight distribution wavers. Pair it with a JP Australia Wave Pro board (95–115 litres depending on your weight) for reliable early planing and crisp response when conditions ease. Finally, protect your core with an ION Seek Lightweight wetsuit so you can focus entirely on technique without cold water distraction. All three pieces work together to optimise your windsurfing light wind sessions. Visit Surf Store to test these models and receive personalised rigging advice from our instructors.

title: Wing Foiling Minimum Wind — Can You Fly in 12 Knots?
meta_title: Wing Foiling Minimum Wind 12–14 Knots Guide 2026
meta_description: Learn wing foiling in 12–14 knot minimum wind. Gear sizes, technique tips, and safety. Find wings and boards at Surf Store.
excerpt: Wing foiling in 12–14 knots is achievable with the right technique, wing size, and board setup—here's what you need to know.
body_html:
Understanding These Conditions

Wing foiling minimum wind sits in a narrow sweet spot: around 12–14 knots is where most riders can just about generate enough lift to foil consistently. Below 12 knots, most standard wings struggle to generate upwind drive and sustained foil time. At 12–14 knots, you're working at the lower boundary—light, variable, often gusty conditions that demand focus and technique.

These conditions are common during spring and autumn mornings, or on days when the forecast looks marginal. The water is usually calm, sometimes glassy, which makes it easier to read wind patterns and learn wing foiling technique without fighting choppy seas.

Wing foiling minimum wind in this range rewards patience. Gusts will lift you into the foil; lulls will drop you back. It's not forgiving, but it teaches you efficiency and board control faster than stronger conditions.

Best Gear for These Conditions

At wing foiling minimum wind levels, your setup is critical. Size up on your wing—most riders use 4.5–5.0 m² wings in 12–14 knots, where lighter conditions might call for 5.0–5.5 m². Brands like Duotone and Cabrinha design wings with excellent low-end torque and responsive turning to maximise lift in marginal breeze.

Your board should be a dedicated foil board: 70–85 litres for most adults, with a high-aspect foil for efficiency in light wind. The thinner rails and longer waterline help you generate glide and reduce the effort needed to stay airborne. A board that's too small will be exhausting; too large and you'll struggle to control weight distribution over the foil.

For a wetsuit, a 3/2 mm spring suit or 4/3 mm winter suit depending on season keeps you flexible and warm during slower sessions. Look for a model with taped seams—every degree counts when you're spending more time in the water learning technique.

Technique Tips

In wing foiling minimum wind, your stance and wing trim make all the difference. Stay forward in your feet, keep your weight centred and slightly forward of the foil. This maintains pressure on the mast and helps generate lift when wind is scarce.

Use broad, smooth strokes with the wing rather than aggressive pumping. The goal is consistent pressure and efficiency. Pump the wing rhythmically—especially during the initial pop and during lulls—to maintain momentum without exhausting yourself.

Tack and jibe with exaggerated smoothness. In light wind, abrupt movements lose drive instantly. Keep your transitions wide and deliberate, maintaining wing pressure throughout. Once foiling, ride as centred as possible; excess weight forward or back kills glide quickly.

Watch your line continuously. Even small gusts are your lifeline. Position yourself to feel wind patterns—ripples on the water, cloud shadows, and wind seam lines are your early warning system.

Safety Checklist

  • Leash always on: In light wind, you'll fall in regularly learning technique. A secure leash keeps your wing and board nearby.
  • Buddy system: Light-wind sessions often mean fewer riders out. Always have a friend nearby or on shore watching.
  • Coast awareness: Check tidal drift and current. In 12–14 knots, you're not generating strong upwind drive, so you may drift downwind faster than you expect.
  • Check the forecast: Wind can drop below foilable conditions. Know what the trend is before you launch.
  • Warm-up gradually: Light-wind sessions involve more frequent dunks. Ease in rather than charging hard immediately.
  • Know your exit: In marginal wind, if you lose foil in a bad spot, identify where you can safely paddle back.

Our Gear Recommendations at Surf Store

For wing foiling minimum wind sessions, we recommend the Duotone Wing Unit in 4.5 m²—a rock-solid, efficient wing designed for light conditions with responsive turning and low-end grunt. Pair it with a JP Australia Foil Board in 75 litres, engineered for early foil pop and smooth glide. For protection in cooler spring and autumn sessions, the ION Wetsuit in 4/3 mm with chest zip keeps you warm and mobile through a long light-wind session. Browse our full selection of wings, foil boards, and wetsuits at Surf Store to find your ideal light-wind setup.

title: Wing Foiling in 20 Knots — Wing Size & Foil Setup Guide
meta_title: Wing Foiling 20 Knots: Best Wing Size & Gear 2026
meta_description: Master wing foiling in 20 knots with our expert guide on wing sizes, technique & safety. Find premium gear at Surf Store.
excerpt: 20 knots is ideal for wing foiling progression. Learn which wing size to choose, how to adjust your technique, and what safety measures matter most.
body_html:
Understanding These Conditions

20 knots is a sweet spot for wing foiling in 20 knots – consistent enough to ride smoothly, yet forgiving enough for learning new tricks and building confidence. This wind speed falls squarely in the intermediate range where most riders find their rhythm. The water is usually manageable with small chop rather than chaotic seas, and you'll have enough power to maintain foiling without fighting the wing constantly.

At this wind strength, the foil stays engaged predictably, gybes feel controlled, and you can experiment with wave riding or freestyle elements without the wing fighting you. It's the wind speed where wing foiling truly becomes fun rather than a struggle – you're not wrestling the wing, and you're not bored waiting for gusts either.

Best Gear for These Conditions
