Visit us to find your ideal wetsuit thickness water temperature match, and don't hesitate to ask—choosing the wrong suit wastes money and ruins sessions.

title: Kite Size by Weight & Wind Speed — Full Calculator Guide
meta_title: Kite Size Calculator by Weight & Wind Speed 2026
meta_description: Learn to choose the right kite size using our weight and wind speed chart. Expert guidance for kitesurfers. Shop now at Surf Store.
excerpt: Match your kite size to your body weight and local wind conditions using a simple chart and three-step formula for safe, responsive riding.
body_html:
The right kite size depends on your body weight and the wind speed at your spot. As a general rule, heavier riders need larger kites in light winds, and lighter riders can use smaller kites in strong winds. Use this kite size calculator approach: find your weight range on a wind speed chart, cross-reference your typical wind conditions, and choose a size that keeps your bar pressure comfortable and your board in the water.

The Full Answer

Choosing a kite size by weight and wind speed isn't rocket science, but it requires understanding how three variables work together: your body mass, wind speed at your location, and the kite's design. A kite size chart is your starting point, but real-world conditions demand a bit of judgment.

Wind speed varies dramatically across Europe—Danish coasts see consistent 12–18 knots, whereas Mediterranean spots can be gusty and unpredictable. Your weight directly affects how much power you need to generate lift and forward motion. Heavier riders (over 90 kg) typically ride 14–17 m² kites in moderate winds; lighter riders (under 70 kg) often prefer 12–14 m² in similar conditions.

A proper kite size calculator factors in:

  • Your body weight (including gear)
  • Wind speed range at your local spot
  • Kite design (wave, freestyle, or freeride)
  • Your skill level and board size

Most manufacturers publish a weight and wind speed chart with their kites. For example, a Duotone Click or Cabrinha Switchblade will list recommended sizes for 65–75 kg riders in 10–15 knots, 15–20 knots, and so on. These charts are built on thousands of hours of testing.

Practical Guide

Step 1: Know Your True Weight

Weigh yourself fully clothed and wearing your wetsuit and harness. This is your riding weight. If you're 75 kg without gear but 82 kg suited up, use 82 kg.

Step 2: Check Your Local Wind Range

Look at historical wind data for your spot. Most European spots average 12–18 knots; some jump to 20+ in winter. If you have no data, ask local instructors or check Windy.com.

Step 3: Cross-Reference a Kite Size Chart

Here's a simple weight and wind speed reference:

Rider Weight (kg) 10–12 Knots 13–16 Knots 17–20 Knots 21+ Knots
60–70 kg 15–17 m² 12–14 m² 10–12 m² 9–11 m²
70–85 kg 16–19 m² 13–15 m² 11–13 m² 10–12 m²
85–100 kg 17–21 m² 14–17 m² 12–14 m² 11–13 m²
100+ kg 19–23 m² 16–19 m² 13–16 m² 12–14 m²

This kite size calculator table works for freeride kites in steady wind. Wave kites are usually 1–2 m² smaller; freestyle kites depend more on your style.

Step 4: Start Conservative

If you're between sizes, choose the smaller one. It's easier to add power (edge harder, move further back on your board) than to manage a kite that's too big.

Common Mistakes

Ignoring Gusts and Lulls – Many beginners buy for average wind and struggle in gusts. A lighter kite size in 16-knot conditions means you can handle 20-knot puffs safely.

Forgetting Gear Weight – Your 75 kg body plus harness, wetsuit, and board adds up. Calculate true riding weight.

Trusting Only One Brand's Chart – A Cabrinha Switchblade and ION Lounge have different designs and handle wind differently. Always check the maker's weight and wind speed chart for that specific kite.

Not Adjusting for Wave vs. Freeride – Wave kites are smaller and more responsive; freeride kites are larger for light wind. Using a wave kite size on a freeride mission means underpowered riding.

Surf Store Recommendation

At Surf Store, we stock Duotone, Cabrinha, NeilPryde, and Point7 kites—each with detailed weight and wind speed charts. We recommend visiting in person or emailing our team with your weight, local wind range, and riding style. We'll help you pick the right size and ensure your entire quiver (kite, bar, board) works together.

If you're shopping online, download each kite manufacturer's official sizing guide and compare. We can also advise on a two-kite quiver (e.g., 14 m² and 17 m²) that covers most European conditions year-round. Browse our range at surf-store.com or contact us for personalised guidance.

title: Windsurf Sail Size per Wind Conditions — How to Choose
meta_title: Windsurf Sail Size Guide by Wind Conditions 2026
meta_description: Learn how to choose the right windsurf sail size for your wind conditions. Expert guide with practical tips. Shop now at Surf Store.
excerpt: Match your windsurf sail size to wind speed and your weight: lighter winds need larger sails (5.0–6.5 m²), medium winds suit 4.5–5.5 m², and strong winds req...
body_html:
Choosing the right windsurf sail size depends on three factors: wind speed, your body weight, and your skill level. Light winds demand larger sails to generate enough power; strong winds require smaller sails to maintain control. A good rule of thumb is that heavier sailors can use slightly larger sails in the same conditions, whilst lighter riders need smaller sizes to stay balanced.

The Full Answer

Your windsurf sail size per wind conditions guide starts with understanding wind range. Wind speed dictates how much surface area you need to harness power. A 7.0 m² sail in 8 knots will feel dead; the same sail in 20 knots will overpower you instantly. Sail size charts exist for this reason—they bridge the gap between conditions and your ability to control the board.

Body weight matters more than many sailors realise. A 60 kg sailor and an 90 kg sailor experience the same 15 knots completely differently. The heavier rider presses down harder, needs more lift to stay up, and benefits from a larger sail. The lighter rider generates speed more easily and feels overpowered sooner. Manufacturers publish weight ranges on sails; these are genuine guides, not marketing fiction.

Skill level is your third variable. Beginners should err on the side of undersizing. A sail that feels slightly underpowered is far safer and more fun than one that constantly threatens to rip your arms off. You'll progress faster learning technique in manageable conditions than fighting a sail that's too big.

Practical Guide

Light winds (8–12 knots): Use 6.0–7.0 m² sails. You're looking for maximum surface area to catch any breeze. Aim for a sail that feels slightly underpowered at the start of your session—you'll feel power build as the wind picks up.

Moderate winds (12–18 knots): Move to 5.0–6.0 m² sails. This is the sweet spot for most sailors. You'll have power, manoeuvrability, and control. Most sailors keep 5.5 m² and 4.5 m² sails as their core quiver.

Strong winds (18–25 knots): Step down to 4.0–5.0 m² sails. Your focus shifts to staying upright and locked in. Smaller sails mean faster response and easier tricks.

Very strong winds (25+ knots): Use 3.5 m² or smaller. These are specialist conditions. Only venture here if you're confident in your footstrap work and can handle choppy water.

Test yourself at the local spot before committing to a full quiver. Borrow or rent sails in different sizes to see how they feel in real conditions. Wind on the water is never the same twice—learning to read it is your most valuable skill.

Common Mistakes

  • Oversizing out of fear: New sailors often buy larger sails thinking they'll have more options. Truth: one oversized sail is useless in everything except light wind. Better to have two well-matched sails covering a narrower range.
  • Ignoring weight guidelines: A 70 kg sailor using a chart designed for 85 kg sailors will feel overpowered. Manufacturer recommendations exist for a reason—follow them.
  • Not accounting for location: Gusty spots need smaller sails than steady-wind bays. The same wind speed feels completely different depending on whether gusts are +3 knots or +8 knots.
  • Holding onto sails too long: Your first 5.5 m² sail served you well, but once you're stronger, it's probably too big for your local spot. Refresh your quiver as your skills improve.

Surf Store Recommendation

We stock premium sails from Duotone, NeilPryde, Cabrinha, and Gaastra—all brands that publish honest wind and weight recommendations. If you're building your first windsurf quiver, start with one 5.5 m² and one 4.5 m² sail. That covers 10–24 knots for most sailors and weighs far less than a full five-sail arsenal.

Visit us to discuss your weight, local wind patterns, and experience level. Our staff have logged thousands of hours on the water and can recommend a sail size that matches your conditions and goals. We also offer rental options so you can trial sizes before buying.

title: Wing Foil Size Guide — Choosing Your Wing by Wind Speed
Kite Foiling in Light Wind — Minimum Wind & Technique Guide
meta_title: Kite Foiling Light Wind Guide 2026 | Surf Store
meta_description: Master kite foiling light wind techniques with the right gear. Learn minimum speed, board selection & safety tips. Shop now at Surf Store.
excerpt: Kite foiling light wind sessions demand the right combination of large kites, lightweight foil boards, and precise body positioning to generate lift and main...
body_html:
Understanding These Conditions

Light wind kite foiling sits in that sweet spot between frustration and pure magic. You're looking at roughly 8–14 knots of consistent wind—the zone where traditional twin-tip riders are barely getting going, but foilers can unlock an entirely different dimension. The key difference is lift: a foil board gets you airborne at speeds where a conventional board would feel sluggish, so you need less apparent wind to keep moving and flying.

These sessions are often the most technical and rewarding. The wind may be gusty or thermals, so your kite foiling light wind technique must account for lulls and micro-adjustments. You're riding a razor's edge between gliding effortlessly and dropping back to the water, which demands focus and finesse. Most serious foilers actually prefer these lighter conditions once they've dialled in their skills—there's less power, more control, and nowhere to hide your technique mistakes.

Best Gear for These Conditions

Kite selection is everything in light wind. You'll want a large platform: 15–17 m² is typical for most adult riders, though lighter or smaller athletes can work with 13–15 m². The Duotone Evo or Cabrinha Switchblade are excellent here because they deliver maximum grunt while staying forgiving. Both are designed with low-end power in mind—they bite hard in marginal wind without feeling oversensitive.

Your foil board must be lightweight and have significant volume. A 70–90 litre directional foil from JP Australia or Fanatic gives you the float and glide-angle you need when the wind drops. The wider, fuller outline keeps your momentum going through lulls. Avoid narrow speed boards for light wind; they'll sink in the gusty patches.

Wing foiling is also gaining traction in these conditions, and you might consider a hybrid approach: a 4.5–5.5 m² wing paired with a foil board offers incredible efficiency in light wind. However, if you're committed to kite foiling, stick with your large kite and dial in your board volume.

Wetsuits matter more than people think. In light wind, you're out longer and your body temperature drops. An ION 4/3 or 5/4 Winter suit keeps you warm and nimble—you can't stay relaxed and precise when you're shivering. Check our isothermal and premium range at Surf Store.

Technique Tips

Your body position is king in light wind. Stay centred over the foil and keep your weight forward to maintain glide. Avoid any sudden movements; every shift of weight affects your lift balance. Weight your front foot slightly during apparent wind shortfalls to keep the foil working efficiently.

Kite foiling light wind riding requires constant micro-adjustments. Keep the kite high in the window (11–1 o'clock position) and use small steering movements rather than aggressive slashes. You're trying to maintain speed through lulls, not chase big manoeuvres. If you lose flight, immediately pump the kite to regain lift—aggressive vertical pumps are your friend.

Use your straps smartly. In light wind, secure foot placement is crucial, so ensure your bindings aren't too tight—you need to move your feet subtly to manage weight distribution. Many foilers drop the back foot strap entirely in these conditions to allow free weight adjustment.

Reading the wind texture is essential. Look for darker patches on the water (wind lines), ride toward them, and position your kite to catch the energy. Light wind often means thermals or shifty patterns, so anticipate lulls and build speed before them.

Safety Checklist

  • Foil depth: Check your foil is secure and your mast isn't bent. A loose mast in light wind can snap, leaving you stranded.
  • Kite inflation: Light-wind kites are larger and may be slightly under-pressurised to improve response. Inspect the bladder for leaks before launching.
  • Downwind drift: In marginal wind, you'll drift faster than you expect. Choose launch spots with clear downwind space and no hazards (rocks, swimmers, cliffs).
  • Hypothermia: Light-wind sessions are long and tiring. Wear a good wetsuit, take breaks, and know your cold tolerance.
  • Recovery strategy: Always have an escape plan. In very light wind, you may not be able to fly back up-wind. Scout shallow water or a safe downwind exit.
  • Buddy system: Foiling in light wind is technical and can feel isolating. Bring a friend, especially if you're learning.

Our Gear Recommendations at Surf Store

For serious light-wind kite foiling, we recommend the Duotone Evo 15 m²—it's our go-to large kite because it feels responsive even in 8-knot thermal thermals and has the low-end grunt competitive foilers demand. Pair it with a JP Australia Freestyle Wave 75L, which floats beautifully, glides forever, and won't feel sluggish in marginal wind.

Don't overlook your wetsuit: our ION Onyx 5/4 Winter is a favourite among European light-wind foilers because it balances warmth, mobility, and durability. Check our complete kite and foil board range at Surf Store for stock and expert advice.

title: Kiteboard Size for Light Wind — Large Board Guide 2026
meta_title: Best Kiteboard Size for Light Wind | 2026 Guide
meta_description: Light wind requires larger kiteboards (15m+) for better float and control. Learn sizing, brands & tips. Shop now at Surf Store.
excerpt: For light wind conditions, choose a kiteboard sized 15m or larger to maximise float, response, and your ability to stay upwind.
body_html:
In light wind, you need a larger kiteboard—typically 15m or bigger—to generate enough float and get moving efficiently. The bigger surface area catches what little wind is available, giving you better control, easier water starts, and the ability to stay upwind. Size matters far more in weak conditions than in strong wind; undersizing in light wind is one of the fastest ways to spend your session swimming.

The Full Answer

Light wind kiteboarding demands a completely different board selection strategy than strong-wind sessions. When we talk about "light wind," most riders mean anything below 12 knots, though the exact threshold depends on your weight, skill level, and the specific board's design.

A larger kiteboard size for light wind isn't just about comfort—it's essential for staying in control. Bigger boards have more surface area and inherent flotation, which means they respond more predictably to small inputs and won't sink beneath you during tricks or manoeuvres. You'll also find that a larger board generates its own momentum more easily, allowing you to maintain speed without constant effort.

Most light-wind specialists ride boards between 15m and 19m depending on bodyweight and skill. Heavier riders, or those weighing over 85kg, often prefer boards at the top end of that range or even larger freestyle-focused shapes that prioritise float. Lighter riders under 70kg can sometimes get away with a 15m or 17m all-rounder, but when the wind drops, more board is nearly always the answer.

Practical Guide

Step 1: Know Your Wind Threshold
Identify the lightest wind you typically encounter at your local spot. If you're regularly faced with sub-10-knot sessions, investing in a dedicated large light-wind board makes sense. If light wind is occasional, a versatile 17m hybrid or freestyle shape might suffice.

Step 2: Match Board Size to Your Bodyweight

  • Under 60kg: start with 15–17m for light wind
  • 60–85kg: 17–19m is your sweet spot
  • Over 85kg: 19m or larger, or consider a dedicated light-wind shape

Step 3: Choose the Right Board Profile
Freestyle and wave boards tend to have less volume and won't float well in light wind. Look for all-rounder or light-wind specific shapes from brands like Duotone, Cabrinha, and Nobile, which are engineered with extra buoyancy and a predictable edge.

Step 4: Test Before You Buy
Rent or borrow a larger board first to feel the difference. Many riders are surprised at how much easier a 19m board is compared to their 17m in marginal wind—this direct comparison builds confidence in the right choice.

Common Mistakes

Going Too Small
Many beginners think "I'll just push harder and learn to work my 15m board in light wind." This leads to frustration: you sink, struggle to get up, and exhaust yourself. A kiteboard size for light wind should always skew larger, not smaller.

Ignoring Water Temperature and Buoyancy Gear
Thick wetsuits and buoyancy vests add flotation, meaning you might get away with a slightly smaller board. Conversely, riding in a summer rashguard alone demands extra board volume. Factor this into your sizing decision.

Confusing Freestyle with Light-Wind Performance
A sleek 13m freestyle board is brilliant in 18+ knots, but in light wind it'll feel like a plank. Light-wind kiteboards need generous volume and a forgiving edge; freestyle performance takes a back seat.

Surf Store Recommendation

For serious light-wind sessions, we recommend Duotone's Evo or Cabrinha's Plasma series—both designed specifically to shine when the wind is thin. If you're under 75kg and want versatility across a range of conditions, Nobile's all-rounder shapes offer excellent light-wind performance without sacrificing speed in moderate wind.

Heavier riders or those hunting maximum float should look at Duotone's larger FSW or Cabrinha's Ace. These boards excel in marginal conditions and hold their edge beautifully when the breeze drops.

Our team at Surf Store can help you dial in the exact kiteboard size light wind demands based on your weight, local conditions, and riding style. Visit us online or contact our experts for a personalised recommendation—we'll make sure you've got the right gear to make the most of every session.

title: Foil vs Regular Kiteboard in Light Wind — Which Is Better?
meta_title: Foil vs Regular Kiteboard Light Wind 2026
meta_description: Discover whether a foil or regular kiteboard performs better in light wind. Expert guidance for kitesurfers. Shop at Surf Store.
excerpt: Foil kiteboards are superior in light wind because they generate lift earlier and require less power, making them ideal when conditions are marginal.
body_html:
In light wind, a foil kiteboard will almost always outperform a regular kiteboard. Foils generate lift at lower speeds, allow you to get going with less wind power, and keep you riding longer as conditions drop. If you're serious about extending your sessions into marginal conditions, foil vs regular kiteboard light wind comparisons consistently show foils winning on early take-off, sustained glide, and overall control.

The Full Answer

A foil kiteboard works fundamentally differently from a directional or twin-tip board. Instead of relying on flotation and the board's shape to stay above water, a hydrofoil—a submerged wing beneath the board—generates hydrodynamic lift. This lift begins working the moment you're moving, even at very low speeds. In light wind, this is a game-changer.

Regular kiteboards (directional or twin-tips) depend on your board's volume and flotation to keep you afloat and moving. When the wind drops below a certain threshold—typically 10–12 knots for most riders on standard boards—you lose the power needed to generate enough board speed. Your kite struggles to relaunch, and you find yourself swimming to retrieve it.

With a foil, that threshold drops significantly. Many experienced foilers can ride comfortably in 8–10 knots, and skilled riders push into even lighter conditions. The foil's lift means you need less apparent wind speed to stay riding, giving you more sessions and longer riding time when conditions are borderline.

Practical Guide

Choosing between foil vs regular kiteboard light wind:

  • Wind range under 12 knots: Use a foil. You'll generate riding speed earlier and maintain it longer as the wind fades.
  • Wind range 12–18 knots: Either works, but foils remain more forgiving. Regular boards are more playful and easier to land tricks.
  • Wind range 18+ knots: Both excel, but regular boards may feel more responsive for freestyle and aggressive riding.

Practical light-wind scenarios:

  • Early morning sessions before the sea breeze builds: Start on a foil, switch to your regular board once wind increases.
  • Late afternoon fades: A foil extends your session by 30–60 minutes in many European coastal conditions.
  • Inland lakes with variable wind: Foils are essential if you want consistent riding across the day.

To maximise your foil's light-wind advantage, use a larger kite (15–17m for most adults) and focus on smooth, efficient movements. Abrupt movements kill speed on foils more than on regular boards.

Common Mistakes

Many beginners assume foil vs regular kiteboard light wind choice comes down to budget alone. Whilst foils are more expensive, they're not just a luxury—they're a capability upgrade. You're not just spending more; you're gaining access to conditions you literally cannot ride on a regular board.

A second mistake: thinking a foil removes the need for solid technique. Foils are incredibly responsive, which means poor weight distribution and jerky movements feel amplified. Light-wind foiling demands smooth, progressive inputs and solid body positioning. Beginners often blame the foil when the issue is technique.

Third: buying a foil that's too small or thin for your wind range. In light wind, you want a larger foil front wing (around 1200–1400 cm²) for maximum early lift. Compact foils excel in strong wind but struggle when conditions are marginal.

Surf Store Recommendation

If you're committed to light-wind riding, we recommend starting with a hybrid approach: invest in one quality foil setup (Duotone or Cabrinha foils paired with a 15–17m kite) and keep a trusted regular board for stronger conditions and freestyle sessions.

Our team at Surf Store specialises in matching the right foil board, mast, and front wing to your weight, skill level, and local wind patterns. We stock Duotone and Cabrinha foil systems trusted by professionals across Europe. For light-wind sessions, we typically suggest pairing a larger kite with a board featuring a generous hydrofoil for maximum efficiency.

Visit surf-store.com to explore our foil and regular kiteboard ranges, or contact our staff directly. We'll help you build a quiver that covers everything from marginal winds to strong conditions, ensuring you're never stuck onshore.

title: Directional vs Twin-Tip in Waves — Which Kiteboard to Choose?
meta_title: Directional vs Twin-Tip Kiteboard in Waves 2026
meta_description: Choose between directional and twin-tip kiteboards for wave riding. Expert guide on which works best for your skill level. Find it at Surf Store.
excerpt: For serious wave riding, a directional kiteboard gives better control and speed, whilst twin-tips suit freestyle and variable wind conditions.
body_html:
For dedicated wave sessions, a directional kiteboard is almost always your better choice. Directional boards lock you into the wave with better hold and drive, whilst twin-tips prioritise tricks and park riding over wave performance.

The Full Answer

The choice between a directional kiteboard and a twin-tip for waves comes down to what you want from your session. A directional kiteboard in waves is purpose-built: it has a narrow nose, wider tail, and continuous rocker that cuts through chop, accelerates down the line, and holds an edge in hollowed sections. Think of it like a surfboard with a footstrap—it's not designed to spin backwards, and it doesn't need to.

Twin-tips, by contrast, are symmetrical for 360-degree riding and freestyle tricks. They excel in flat water and light winds where you can manipulate them in any direction. But in waves, that symmetry becomes a liability: they're less responsive to weight shifts, slower to carve, and they don't generate the speed you need to punch out or link turns down a proper swell.

Your skill level matters too. Beginners on a twin-tip struggle to angle and control their board through a whitewater section. On a directional kiteboard in waves, even a novice feels more locked in and confident. Intermediate and advanced riders notice the difference immediately—better speed control, sharper bottom turns, and the ability to hold tight lines without fighting the board.

Practical Guide

If you're a beginner or intermediate rider: Use a directional board in anything resembling a wave or swell. Start with a board 1–2 litres smaller than your twin-tip so it remains responsive, then progress to narrower outlines as your technique improves. A directional kiteboard in waves between 0.5–1.5 metres is your sweet spot for learning proper technique without chasing loose equipment.

If you're an advanced rider: You'll want multiple quivers. Keep a wave-focused directional board (typically 39–47 cm wide, 55–65 litres) for swell sessions, and a freestyle twin-tip for flat-spell creativity. Many committed wave riders also use smaller directional boards (sub-50 litres) in stormy conditions where volume matters less than drive and edge hold.

Board size matters more than shape. A slightly undersized directional board outperforms an oversized twin-tip in waves every time. Downsize by 5–10 litres from your usual twin-tip volume—you'll carve tighter and feel the swell beneath your feet instead of floating over it.

Test before you buy. If you're not sure, visit Surf Store and speak with our team. We have rental equipment and real water experience to match you with the right directional kiteboard for your local conditions.

Common Mistakes

  • Staying on your twin-tip because you're comfortable. Comfort isn't progression. Switching to a proper directional kiteboard forces you to refine your technique and accelerates your learning by months.
  • Buying a directional board that's too big. Volume is not your friend in waves. A board that floats you also resists your turns. Go smaller.
  • Ignoring your local wind window. A directional kiteboard in waves demands consistent wind to generate speed. In light, gusty conditions, a twin-tip's forgiveness pays off.
  • Assuming all directional boards are the same. Wave-specific shapes differ hugely. A directional board designed for light wind (flat rocker, wide tail) will disappoint in choppy, powerful conditions. Match the board to your break.
  • Not adjusting your stance. Directional riding requires you to angle your board slightly sideways and use your back foot to drive. Beginners often stand square and wonder why their board won't carve.

Surf Store Recommendation

Our specialist team recommends starting with a wave-focused directional model from Duotone, Cabrinha, or Fanatic—all available at Surf Store in a range of sizes and price points. If you're unsure whether a directional kiteboard is right for you, book a lesson or rental session with us first. We stock wetsuits from ION to keep you warm in European swells, and our staff have years of on-water experience to guide you towards the right kit for your goals and your break. Shop kiteboards at surf-store.com and upgrade your wave riding today.

title: Kite Harness Setup for Big Air & Jumping — Settings Guide
meta_title: Kite Harness for Jumping Big Air 2026 | Surf Store
meta_description: Master big air jumping with proper kite harness settings. Learn technique, gear, and safety for massive airs. Find it at Surf Store.
excerpt: Discover the essential kite harness adjustments, technique, and gear needed to land massive big air jumps safely and consistently.
body_html:
Understanding These Conditions

Big air jumping demands strong, consistent wind—typically 14 knots minimum, though 16–22 knots is ideal for learning. The wind needs to be stable; thermals and gusts will work against you. Look for offshore or cross-shore wind patterns without chop, ideally a flat-water lagoon or bay where you can generate maximum height without fighting waves.

Water temperature matters too. Jumping requires explosive movement and recovery from falls, so a quality wetsuit keeps you warm and mobile during extended sessions. Air temperature should allow for extended effort without fatigue setting in quickly.

Best Gear for These Conditions

Your kite harness for jumping big air needs to prioritise comfort and control above all else. A waist harness (rather than seat) gives you freedom of movement and better body rotation. Look for a harness with adequate back support—something like the Duotone or Cabrinha models that distribute load evenly across your core.

Kite size typically ranges from 14m to 17m, depending on wind strength and your weight. In 16–18 knot wind, a 14m is perfect for progressive jumps. Lighter riders may prefer 12m for responsiveness. Boards should be small and lightweight—36–41 litres—so you're not fighting resistance during take-off. A modern freestyle board from Nobile or Fanatic gives you the pop needed to launch.

Choose a chest-protector wetsuit (3/2mm) with reinforced shoulders and back. ION specialises in impact protection; their vests keep you safe on hard landings whilst allowing full mobility. Thick, flexible material in the harness contact zone prevents chafing during multiple jumps.

Technique Tips

Your kite harness for big air setup must allow you to load and unload bar pressure smoothly. Sit slightly back in your harness during the pop phase—this shifts your weight towards the tail and lets your legs drive upward. As you jump, your harness should feel like a stable platform, not a restriction.

Before launching, adjust your harness so the hook sits at mid-chest height. This position gives you the best mechanical advantage when you sheet in hard for the jump. Your hands grip the bar with the back hand ready to release tension at the peak of your air—this is where harness height matters; a poorly positioned hook forces awkward arm angles.

During flight, lean your body slightly forward to maintain kite control. Your shoulders should stay loose; tension in the harness translates to tension in your arms, which kills style and control. Land with bent knees, land slightly switch (tail-forward) if possible, and immediately work the kite to absorb impact.

Safety Checklist

  • Harness inspection: Check all stitching, the hook mechanism, and webbing for wear before every session. A failed harness at height is dangerous.
  • Impact risk: Always wear a helmet and chest protector. Big airs mean big falls. ION vests are standard for a reason—they save ribs and shoulders.
  • Buddy system: Never jump alone. Have a spotter on shore or on a board nearby who can call conditions and watch for your safety.
  • Wind stability: If wind gusts above 25 knots or drops below 12 knots suddenly, stop jumping. Inconsistent wind throws off your timing and launch angles.
  • Depower setup: Your harness hook should allow quick bar release. Test this on land; if your bar won't release smoothly, adjust hook angle or bar trim.
  • Fatigue awareness: Jumping is physically demanding. Stop before you're exhausted; tired riders crash harder.

Our Gear Recommendations at Surf Store

At Surf Store, we stock dedicated big air harnesses from market leaders. The Duotone Warp Harness is engineered for jumping—it offers a low hook position, reinforced back panel, and waist straps that won't slip during explosive movement. It's stiff where it needs to be and flexible where you need mobility.

For protection, the ION Collision Vest combines neoprene impact absorption with lightweight design. It won't restrict your range of motion, and it'll save you from serious injury on hard landings.

Pair these with a responsive board like the Nobile NHP Wave Board (36–41L depending on your weight). The rocker and flex profile are tuned for quick pop and stable flight. We've tested all these products on the water; they deliver real results.

Visit Surf Store to browse our full selection of kite harnesses, impact protection, and freestyle gear. Our team has decades of combined jumping experience—we're happy to advise on the right setup for your goals and conditions.

title: Windsurf Harness Settings for Strong Wind — Tuning Guide
meta_title: Windsurf Harness Strong Wind Tips 2026 | Surf Store
meta_description: Master windsurf harness settings for strong wind conditions. Expert tips on technique, gear selection & safety. Find it at Surf Store.
excerpt: Learn how to adjust your windsurf harness, technique, and gear selection when strong wind strikes your local spot.
body_html:
Understanding These Conditions

Strong wind on the water—typically 25+ knots—demands respect and preparation. When conditions like these arrive, your windsurf harness becomes far more than a comfort device; it's a critical tool for energy management, board control, and injury prevention. Strong wind sessions push both your body and equipment to their limits, so understanding what you're facing is the first step toward staying safe and having fun.

Strong wind means overpowered conditions where even experienced riders feel the pull of the sail. Your legs, core, and shoulders will fatigue faster, and a poorly adjusted windsurf harness will accelerate that exhaustion or, worse, cause injury. The key is distributing load intelligently across your body and choosing a harness type that suits the intensity of these sessions.

Best Gear for These Conditions

For strong wind, sail size is your first consideration. Most riders drop to 3.5–4.5 m² waves sails or use small freestyle/freeride sails designed for control and durability. Larger wave sails naturally encourage smaller harness loads through better wave performance; smaller freestyle sails demand more harness reliance, so choose accordingly.

Your windsurf harness settings matter enormously here. In strong wind, move your boom height slightly lower than usual—this increases downward pull and helps anchor you to the board rather than being lifted. Position your harness lines so the hook sits directly under your centre of gravity; too high and you'll feel pitched backward; too low and you lose power control.

Board choice: narrow wave boards (70–80 litres) are ideal because they carve cleanly and respond instantly to weight shifts. Wider freeride boards (85–95 litres) suit beginners in strong wind because they forgive sloppy footwork and stay more stable.

For harness settings in strong wind, we recommend a waist harness over a seat harness in these conditions. Waist harnesses like our ION Harness Neo offer superior mobility for explosive movements and quick footwork changes—essential when you're battling 30+ knots. The distributed padding protects your lower back during powerful gybes and bottom turns.

Wetsuit: a 4/3 mm or 5/4 mm winter suit with reinforced shoulders from ION Wetsuits is non-negotiable. Strong wind sessions involve frequent wipeouts and intense physical effort; inadequate thermal protection will drain energy and slow recovery.

Technique Tips

Harness technique in strong wind differs significantly from light-air sailing. First, trust your harness. Many riders instinctively grip the boom with their hands, defeating the whole purpose of the harness and tiring themselves faster. Instead, let the harness absorb sail pressure; use your hands only for boom control and direction changes.

Keep your hips forward and your weight centred over the board. In strong wind, the sail wants to lift you; counter this by driving your hips toward the mast foot. This posture also improves carving and prevents the common mistake of getting pinned back on the tail.

Your windsurf harness settings strong wind technique should emphasise hook lock engagement. As pressure builds, actively press your lower abdomen into the hook rather than hanging passively. This gives you dynamic control and lets you modulate power by changing your body angle relative to the sail.

For strong wind gybes, move your harness hand (the front hand) further back on the boom beforehand. This shortens your effective boom length and reduces leverage, making the gybe response less violent and more controllable. After the gybe, slide your hand back forward once you've completed your turn and stabilised on the new tack.

Wave riding in strong wind demands quick transitions between power and float phases. Ease off the harness slightly as you approach waves (allowing arm strength to dominate) so you can carve aggressively. Return to harness lock once you're riding down the wave face.

Safety Checklist

  • Harness fit: ensure your harness is snug with no twisting or migration during aggressive manoeuvres. A loose harness in strong wind can cause back strain or let you slip during critical moments.
  • Impact protection: strong wind sessions mean frequent falls. Wear impact shorts or a rash vest with padding to protect tailbone and hips.
  • Boom height: too high and you'll be yanked upward on gusts; too low and you lose leverage. Set it at mid-chest height as your baseline.
  • Line tension: check that harness lines are parallel and evenly tensioned. Asymmetrical lines will twist your torso and cause muscle imbalances.
  • Buddy system: never windsurf strong wind sessions alone. A second person onshore or nearby is essential for rescue if needed.
  • Exit strategy: know your exit point before launching. Strong wind can push you downwind faster than expected, leaving you stranded if you're unfamiliar with the coastline.
  • Equipment inspection: check your boom, mast, and harness for cracks or wear before each session. Strong wind amplifies stress on weak points.

Our Gear Recommendations at Surf Store

At Surf Store, we stock the harnesses and sails trusted by European strong wind specialists. For waist harness comfort in demanding conditions, the ION Harness Neo offers premium padding and a secure lock mechanism—ideal for riders who demand reliability during intense sessions. Its low-profile design won't interfere with your footwork, and the ergonomic back panel supports your spine during those powerful bottom turns.

For small sails in strong wind, our Duotone Wave Sails are lightweight, durable, and designed to perform in overpowered conditions. These sails encourage active carving over brute force, letting your harness work smarter rather than harder.

Pair your harness with NeilPryde or Duotone booms in the 140–160 cm range; shorter booms reduce sail area and make harness management more intuitive in strong wind. Browse our full selection of windsurf harnesses and sails to find the perfect setup for your strong wind style.

title: Stuck in a Kitesurfing Rut? — How to Progress Faster
meta_title: Kitesurfing Progress Tips: Improve Faster in 2026
meta_description: Master kitesurfing faster with expert tips on technique, equipment, wind conditions, and training. Break through plateaus at Surf Store.
excerpt: Progress faster in kitesurfing by combining consistent practice in variable wind, deliberate technique work, proper equipment progression, and learning from ...
body_html:
The fastest way to progress in kitesurfing is through consistent practice in varied conditions, deliberate focus on technique rather than distance, and investing in equipment that matches your current skill level rather than jumping ahead. Most serious riders improve dramatically within 6–12 months when they combine regular water time with structured feedback from instructors or experienced peers.

The Full Answer

Kitesurfing progress isn't linear, and plateaus are completely normal. The riders who break through them fastest share three things: they get on the water frequently (ideally 3–4 sessions per week), they actively seek feedback on their technique, and they upgrade their kit strategically rather than randomly.

Your first year is about building foundational skills—launching, landing, controlling power, and staying upwind. These aren't glamorous, but they're the bedrock. Once you're comfortable cruising and turning, your next phase focuses on tricks, edge control, and reading the wind. The mistake many riders make is trying to run before they can walk: buying a massive freestyle kite before they've mastered their first board, or targeting technical tricks when their basic transitions aren't smooth.

Wind conditions matter enormously. Consistent thermal winds (spring and summer afternoons) are ideal for practice because they're predictable. Light winds force you to be precise with your technique—every movement counts. Strong winds are less forgiving but teach respect for the kite. The riders who progress fastest practise across a range of conditions rather than waiting for their perfect 12-knot window.

Practical Guide

Phase 1: Foundation (Sessions 1–30) Focus entirely on control and safety. Launch, land, and simple transitions. Don't worry about style or tricks. Use equipment your instructor recommends, not what you think looks cool. A stable, forgiving board and a mid-range all-rounder kite (like a Duotone Rebel or Cabrinha Switchblade) will teach you far more than aspirational gear.

Phase 2: Consistency (Sessions 31–100) You're now comfortable cruising. This is where deliberate practice accelerates improvement. Film yourself. Ask experienced riders for one piece of feedback per session. Work on upwind riding, smooth transitions, and staying powered. Your equipment choice should now reflect your developing style—if you're gravitating toward freestyle, add a freestyle-specific board to your quiver.

Phase 3: Specialisation (Sessions 100+) Once you're solid at the basics, pick your direction: waves, freestyle, freeride, foiling. Each discipline demands different kit. A NeilPryde Carve Wave kite and wave board are completely different tools from a Point7 freestyle setup. Specialising allows you to invest in better equipment for your chosen discipline and train with peers who share your goals.

Practical steps:

  • Session 1: Book a lesson with a qualified instructor if you're new. This saves months of mistakes.
  • Sessions 2–20: Practise in light winds (8–14 knots) where mistakes are safer and feedback is clearer.
  • Sessions 21–50: Add variety—try different boards, practice in 15–18 knots, film yourself turning.
  • Sessions 51+: Pick a specific skill (powered transitions, unhooked, wave riding) and drill it weekly.
  • Throughout: Spend time on shore—understand weather, wind patterns, and tides. Mental preparation is half the game.

Common Mistakes

Upgrading too early: Beginners often buy a 'bigger' kite thinking it'll help them in light wind. It won't—you'll lose control and build bad habits. Stick with your starting kite until you can launch, land, and basic transition smoothly.

Chasing tricks instead of technique: Every advanced rider you see did thousands of hours of basic carving, edging, and transitions first. Tricks sit on top of solid fundamentals. Skip the foundation and you'll plateau hard.

Ignoring wind conditions: Practising only in strong wind teaches you to musclepower. Light wind forces precision. Ideally, you need both. A rider who can kitesurfing progress tips improve faster plateau effectively in 10-knot conditions will dominate when the wind picks up.

Going it alone: Isolation kills progress. Find a local spot with other riders, share video feedback, and learn from their mistakes too.

Surf Store Recommendation

At Surf Store, we recommend starting with a Duotone Rebel or Cabrinha Switchblade (mid-sized all-rounder kites) paired with a stable freestyle or freeride board like a Fanatic Freewave or JP Australia freestyle model. These tools are forgiving enough to learn on but responsive enough to reward proper technique.

Once you've logged 50+ sessions and know your style, our team can advise on discipline-specific upgrades: wave-specific kites, foiling wings, or freestyle boards from Nobile, Point7, and Tabou. We also stock ION wetsuits that keep you comfortable across European seasons, so you can practise year-round without compromise.

Most importantly, visit our shop or contact our staff. We have real on-water experience and can honestly assess where your equipment is holding you back—and where it isn't. Kitesurfing progress tips improve faster plateau outcomes improve dramatically when your kit is dialled.

title: How to Progress Faster in Windsurfing — Breaking Through the Plateau
First Time Kitesurfing in Open Water — What to Expect
meta_title: Kitesurfing First Time Ocean Tips 2026 | Surf Store
meta_description: Essential kitesurfing first time ocean tips: safety checks, conditions, gear setup. Start confidently. Shop at Surf Store.
excerpt: Before your first kitesurfing session in open water, ensure you have proper training, check weather and tides, inspect your gear thoroughly, and never go alone.
body_html:
Your first kitesurfing session in open water is thrilling but demands respect for conditions and safety fundamentals. Before you launch, you need certified instruction, a thorough pre-launch gear check, local knowledge of wind and tide patterns, and a clear understanding of your limits and rescue protocols.

The Full Answer

Transitioning from a lagoon or flat-water training ground to open ocean kitesurfing marks a significant step in your progression. The stakes are higher: currents run stronger, waves demand respect, and escape routes aren't always obvious. Many beginners focus only on technique and overlook the environmental factors that make open water fundamentally different from protected spots.

Before you go, establish these foundations: completed IKO or equivalent certification (minimum Level 2), personal comfort with your kite in 12–15 knot winds, and honest assessment of your swimming ability in rough conditions. Open water introduces rip currents, variable wind windows, and wave impact—all manageable once you know what to expect and how to respond.

Your first time kitesurfing in open water should happen with a guide, mentor, or instructor present. This isn't timidity; it's the standard practised by top riders worldwide. A second set of eyes catches what you'll miss: deteriorating conditions, approaching swimmers, or early signs you're drifting offshore.

Practical Guide

Check the Forecast and Local Conditions

Wind should be stable at 12–18 knots for your first open-water session. Check tide tables and speak with local riders about current patterns, hazards (rocks, kelp, shipping lanes), and entry/exit points. A beach with onshore wind and gentle swell is ideal; avoid offshore wind or large waves until you've built experience.

Inspect Your Gear Thoroughly

  • Kite: Check all bridle lines for fraying or wear; pump the bladder and listen for hisses; deploy the kite on land and observe symmetry.
  • Board: Inspect for dings, loose bindings, or soft spots in the core.
  • Harness: Ensure the waist is snug, straps aren't twisted, and the hook is clean and functional.
  • Helmet and impact vest: Non-negotiable in open water; they keep you conscious if you fall hard.
  • Leash: Test the carabiner attachment and ensure it's rated for kite use.

Plan Your Route and Communication

Brief a friend onshore: where you're launching, expected duration, and your planned downwind zone. Wear bright clothing and carry a whistle. On your first kitesurfing session in open water, stay within visual range of shore and avoid shipping lanes or populated swimming areas. A GPS watch or smartphone tracker adds reassurance.

Master Relaunch Before You Need It

If your kite crashes in a lagoon, retrieving it is inconvenient. In open ocean, a waterlogged kite is a liability. Practice water relaunch extensively before going out. Know your kite's sweet spot and how to pop it back to life with minimal effort.

Common Mistakes

Overestimating Your Ability

Certification and flat-water proficiency don't translate directly to ocean conditions. Currents, chop, and fatigue compound mistakes. Your first kitesurfing in open water should feel controlled and manageable, not stretched to your limit.

Ignoring Wind Direction and Tide

Launching in offshore wind creates a false sense of security—then suddenly the wind switches or dies, and you're drifting away from shore. Always establish an onshore or cross-shore wind. Check tide timing; launching one hour before slack tide is riskier than launching during the turn.

Skipping the Buddy System

Solo kitesurfing is common among experienced riders in familiar spots. As a beginner, it's risky. Your first time kitesurfing in open water, bring a friend or instructor.

Neglecting Hypothermia Risk

Cold water drains energy fast. A 5 mm wetsuit feels adequate in a 16°C lagoon but becomes critical in a 12°C ocean with waves. Your first session, choose water temperature and thickness conservatively.

Surf Store Recommendation

At Surf Store, we stock everything you need for safe open-water progression. Our ION 5 mm wetsuits deliver warmth and mobility for Atlantic and Northern European conditions. For your first kitesurfing adventure, pair it with a Duotone Impact Vest—lightweight, durable, and recognised across the European kitesurfing community. We also carry Cabrinha and NeilPryde kites known for predictable relaunch and forgiving arc in variable wind. Before you launch, visit us in-store or online; our staff have years of open-water experience and can review your gear setup, discuss local spot conditions, and recommend the right kit for your skill level. Don't hesitate to ask—we'd rather spend 20 minutes preparing you properly than hear you got into trouble because something wasn't checked.

title: How to Kitesurf Upwind Faster — Technique & Tips
meta_title: Kitesurf Upwind Faster: Technique Tips 2026
meta_description: Master how to kitesurf upwind faster with proven technique tips. Learn efficient body position, kite control & more. Shop gear at Surf Store.
excerpt: Efficient upwind kitesurfing depends on precise kite angles, board positioning, and body weight distribution to maximise forward progress against the wind.
body_html:
The key to kitesurfing upwind faster is maintaining the kite in the power zone while keeping your board at a narrow angle to the wind, using your body weight to drive the board forward rather than relying on raw kite power. Most beginners waste energy by pointing too high into the wind or allowing the kite to collapse—instead, you need smooth, rhythmic kite movements combined with aggressive heel-edge pressure to cut through the water efficiently.

The Full Answer

Kitesurfing upwind is fundamentally about three interconnected elements: kite positioning, body mechanics, and board angle. When you want to kitesurf upwind faster, you're fighting natural physics—the wind wants to push you downwind—so every movement must be deliberate and efficient.

The kite should live in the power zone, roughly between 45° and 11 o'clock when facing upwind. This is where the kite generates both lift and drive without stalling. Many riders make the mistake of flying the kite too high (near zenith), where wind speed increases but power diminishes. Instead, keep it lower and let it work.

Your board angle matters enormously. Rather than pointing the nose directly into the wind, angle it approximately 45° to the wind direction—this is your optimal upwind course. Pointing higher wastes speed; pointing lower takes you downwind. Your stance should be athletic: bent knees, weight centred, ready to edge hard on your heels. The heel edge is your engine; it's what keeps you climbing into the wind when the kite alone cannot.

Body weight distribution is how to kitesurf upwind more efficiently. Lean back slightly, keep your shoulders open to the wind, and concentrate pressure through your heels. This position loads the board's rail and allows you to carve smooth, powered turns without losing elevation.

Practical Guide

Step 1: Position Your Kite Correctly

Before you leave the beach, visualise the power zone. Once riding, maintain the kite between 10 and 2 o'clock (if 12 o'clock is directly upwind). Make small, controlled pumps—subtle figure-eights or small loops—to keep energy in the kite without over-committing and crashing.

Step 2: Find Your Optimal Board Angle

Start at 45° to the wind. Feel how the board responds. If you're drifting downwind, angle slightly higher (closer to 30°). If you're barely moving forward, you're probably pointed too high and losing drive. The sweet spot changes with wind strength and your skill level, so experiment in each session.

Step 3: Master Heel-Edge Pressure

This is the core of how to kitesurf upwind faster. Lock your back foot down, engage your heels into the water, and feel the board bite. Your quadriceps and glutes should be working hard. The kite provides lift; your edge provides forward momentum. Together, they climb upwind.

Step 4: Use Smooth Kite Movements

Avoid sudden, aggressive movements. Instead, use rhythm: pump the kite gently as you edge, release slightly as you turn, then pump again as you re-engage. This rhythm keeps you moving steadily without burning out your arms.

Common Mistakes

  • Flying the kite too high: The zenith position looks powerful but provides minimal forward drive. Keep it in the working window.
  • Pointing too high into the wind: Beginners chase height and stall. Aim for 45° and accept that you'll make slower progress but more consistent progress.
  • Relying only on kite power: Many riders expect the kite to drag them upwind. It won't. Your edge, pressure, and body mechanics do 60% of the work.
  • Tensing up: Tight muscles fatigue quickly. Stay relaxed, breathe, and let your edge work for you.
  • Neglecting board selection: A board with poor upwind drive will frustrate even expert technique. Narrower boards with good rail definition climb better.

Surf Store Recommendation

To improve your upwind performance, start with the right equipment. We stock Duotone and Cabrinha kites—both brands excel at responsive bar control and stable power in the window, making how to kitesurf upwind faster much more achievable. For boards, explore Fanatic and Nobile models; both offer designs specifically tuned for upwind efficiency with progressive rail shapes and optimised volumes.

If you're serious about mastering upwind technique, consider a lesson or consultation with our expert staff at Surf Store. We have years of on-water experience and can diagnose exactly where your technique needs refinement. Visit surf-store.com to browse our full range and book a session with us.

title: How to Jump Kitesurfing — First Jump Guide for Beginners
meta_title: How to Jump Kitesurfing First Time | 2026 Guide
meta_description: Learn how to jump kitesurfing safely as a beginner. Master technique, timing, and kite control. Find expert gear at Surf Store.
excerpt: Your first kitesurfing jump comes from combining proper kite control, board positioning, and explosive leg power at the right moment.
body_html:
Your first kitesurfing jump comes down to three things: enough wind, solid edge control, and knowing exactly when to load and release your kite. Most beginners can achieve a small hop once they've mastered basic riding—it's less about strength and more about timing and technique. The jump happens when you harness the kite's power at the peak of your edge, then extend your legs explosively whilst the kite pulls you skyward.

The Full Answer

Learning how to jump kitesurfing builds on skills you've already developed. You need to be comfortable riding upwind, maintaining board control, and responding to kite movements. A first jump is typically just 30–60 centimetres off the water, but it teaches you the fundamental feeling of leaving the surface and landing safely.

The mechanism is straightforward: as you ride on a good edge, you load your board by bending your knees and pulling yourself hard towards the kite. This creates tension. Simultaneously, you move the kite aggressively upwind (towards 12 o'clock). The kite's upward pull combines with your leg extension, lifting you clear of the water. The board stays beneath your feet because your feet remain in the straps. On landing, you absorb impact with bent knees and immediately redistribute your weight across the board's edge.

Wind strength matters. Most riders learn to jump in 12–18 knots of wind. Lighter conditions don't give the kite enough grunt; stronger winds are harder to control when you're new to jumping.

Practical Guide

Step 1: Choose Your Moment

Pick a flat-water spot with consistent wind. Avoid choppy conditions—they destabilise your board mid-jump. Find a time when the wind is steady, not gusty.

Step 2: Build Your Edge

Ride hard on your heel edge, angled away from the kite. Your body should be low, knees bent, hands relaxed on the bar. This creates the tension you'll release.

Step 3: Load and Pop

As you're riding edge, pull the bar towards your hip firmly and bend your knees even deeper—this is the "load". Then, explosively straighten your legs whilst sending the kite to 12 o'clock on the window. The combination of board spring and kite pull lifts you upward.

Step 4: Leave the Water

You'll rise. Keep your feet in the straps and body compact. Look toward your landing zone. Your legs should stay slightly bent, ready to absorb landing.

Step 5: Land and Ride

As your board touches water, drop your hips and flex your knees hard to absorb the impact. Immediately edge again to stabilise. You've landed your first jump.

Common Mistakes

Jumping Too Early – Many beginners try jumping before their riding is solid. If you can't ride a straight line and transition smoothly, jumping is harder. Spend 3–4 weeks on basic riding first.

Not Loading Enough – A weak edge or shallow knee bend means no energy to pop. You must genuinely load the board before extending.

Kite Movement Too Slow – Moving the kite from 10 or 11 o'clock to 12 o'clock must be quick and decisive. Hesitant kite movements won't generate lift.

Stiff Landings – Landing with straight legs jars your knees and throws you off balance. Always land bent and ready to absorb shock.

Poor Wind Choice – Learning to jump in 8 knots is nearly impossible; 25+ knots is chaotic. Aim for 12–16 knots when starting out.

Surf Store Recommendation

Your first jump is easier with the right kit. We stock Duotone and Cabrinha kites—both provide excellent response and predictable power delivery, crucial when you're learning jumps. A Duotone Rebel or Cabrinha Switchblade in 14–17 m² gives you plenty of leverage without being overwhelming.

Board choice matters too. A wider, more buoyant directional board (like models from JP Australia or Fanatic) forgives mistakes and provides better float for learning. Pair it with an ION impact vest—protection builds confidence and lets you practise without fear.

Visit surf-store.com to explore our range of beginner-friendly kites, boards, and protective gear. Our team has years of on-water experience and can recommend exactly what suits your weight, wind conditions, and progression pace.

title: How to Tack in Kitesurfing — Transition Technique Guide
meta_title: How to Tack in Kitesurfing 2026 | Transition Technique Guide
meta_description: Master the kitesurfing tack technique with our step-by-step guide. Learn transitions, avoid common mistakes, and ride better. Shop quality kites at Surf...
excerpt: A tack in kitesurfing is a 180° board rotation where you change direction by turning into the wind and switching feet and kite position.
body_html:
A tack in kitesurfing is a 180° directional change executed by turning your board into the wind whilst switching your feet and moving your kite to the opposite side of the wind window. Unlike a jibe—which sends you downwind—a tack brings you upwind and is essential for navigating narrow channels, returning to launch spots, and building control over your board. Mastering this transition technique separates casual riders from progressing water sports enthusiasts.

The Full Answer

The tack is one of two primary turning techniques in kitesurfing, and understanding the how to transition tack kitesurfing technique properly will transform your riding. When you tack, you're essentially carving your board upwind whilst repositioning your kite to maintain tension and lift. The kite moves from one side of the wind window to the other, creating the power needed to drive your new direction.

This differs fundamentally from a jibe. In a jibe, you turn downwind and the kite follows a wide arc at the edge of the window. A tack, by contrast, moves the kite more aggressively through the centre of the window, demanding quicker reactions and precise timing. Intermediate and advanced riders use tacks repeatedly during a session—cutting back against the wind, repositioning after tricks, or simply changing course without losing board control.

The technique relies on four key elements: board pressure and carving, kite movement through the power zone, weight transfer, and foot positioning. Get any one of these wrong and you'll either stall mid-turn or lose power and wash out.

Practical Guide

Step 1: Set Up Your Approach

Ride straight on a beam reach (perpendicular to wind direction) with your kite at 45° on one side of the window. Build speed so you have momentum and control. Your weight should be centred over the board, feet roughly hip-width apart.

Step 2: Initiate the Carve

Turn your shoulders and head toward the wind. Pressure your front foot to initiate an upwind carve. As your board pivots, keep your kite steady—don't move it yet. This plants your edge and starts the rotation.

Step 3: Move the Kite

As you're 90° through the turn, aggressively move your kite toward the opposite side of the window. Push the bar forward or move it across to the new side, keeping kite height at roughly 45°. This moment is critical: moving the kite too early loses board pressure; too late and you'll stall.

Step 4: Switch Your Feet

Cross your old back foot over to become your new front foot. Keep your knees bent and your core engaged. This foot switch happens naturally as the board completes its 180° rotation—don't overthink it.

Step 5: Trim and Drive

As you complete the turn and the kite gains power, sink your hips into the new direction. Extend through your legs and sheet in (pull the bar) to generate forward momentum on your new heading.

Common Mistakes

  • Moving the Kite Too Early: Beginners often shift the kite before carving, which kills board pressure and causes the board to slip sideways. Keep kite stable until you're 90° into the turn.
  • Leaving the Kite at 12 O'Clock: A kite directly overhead generates no power. Always move it to 45° on the new side—this maintains tension through the transition.
  • Poor Weight Distribution: Leaning back during the turn destabilises you. Stay centred, then gradually transfer weight to your new back foot as you exit.
  • Slow Carve Entry: Without enough speed and commitment, the board won't pivot cleanly. Approach tacks with purpose and assertiveness, especially when learning.
  • Rigid Foot Positioning: Cramping your stance or refusing to switch feet causes imbalance. Allow your hips and shoulders to guide a relaxed, natural foot swap.

Surf Store Recommendation

Mastering the tack requires the right equipment. At Surf Store, we stock responsive, highly manoeuvrable kites from Duotone, Cabrinha, and NeilPryde—all proven platforms for learning and refining transitions. Models like the Duotone Rebel and Cabrinha Drifter excel at staying powered through direction changes, giving you the confidence to commit to the technique.

Pair your kite with a directional or hybrid board from Fanatic or Nobile, which carve upwind efficiently and respond immediately to pressure shifts. We also recommend lighter wind sessions (12–18 knots) while you build muscle memory—our expert staff can advise on conditions and kit matching. Visit surf-store.com to browse our full range or contact us for personalised guidance on tack-friendly setups across Europe.

title: How to Gybe in Kitesurfing — Step-by-Step Technique Guide
meta_title: How to Gybe in Kitesurfing: Technique & Steps 2026
meta_description: Learn how to gybe in kitesurfing with our step-by-step guide. Master this essential technique safely. Shop kite gear at Surf Store.
excerpt: Gybing in kitesurfing is a controlled 180° turn where you redirect your kite and board to change direction, done by flying the kite overhead and shifting you...
body_html:
A gybe in kitesurfing is a controlled 180° turn that lets you reverse direction while staying powered and moving. It's performed by flying your kite overhead, shifting your stance, and redirecting your board downwind. Once you can gybe smoothly, you'll unlock freedom on the water—no more riding in one direction or stopping to reposition.

The Full Answer

Gybing (also spelled jibing) is one of the core manoeuvres in kitesurfing. Unlike a tack, where you turn towards the wind, a gybe takes you away from the wind and is generally faster and more natural for most riders. The gybe lets you change direction by 180° while maintaining pressure on your kite and forward momentum.

The technique works because your kite remains powered throughout the turn. You fly it to the edge of the wind window (overhead), keep tension on the bar, and use the kite's movement to help rotate your board. Your body weight shifts from your back foot to your front foot as you complete the turn, and your kite swings across the zenith—the highest point directly above.

How to gybe in kitesurfing requires coordination between kite control and board positioning. The beauty is that once you grasp the rhythm, it becomes almost second nature. Many riders find gybing easier than tacking because the wind window supports the manoeuvre naturally.

Practical Guide

Step 1: Build Speed and Position
Ride at moderate to good speed on your current tack. Ensure you have open water ahead—no obstacles or other riders. Position yourself so you're heading downwind or slightly across the wind.

Step 2: Redirect Your Kite
Pull your front hand (the one on the same side as your front foot) to move the kite towards the edge of the wind window. If you're going right, pull your right hand to move the kite to the right side of the window. Move it steadily towards the edge and then overhead.

Step 3: Maintain Kite Tension
As the kite moves overhead, keep subtle pressure on the bar. This is where many beginners fail—they release too much tension and lose control. Your kite should feel responsive, not slack.

Step 4: Initiate the Board Turn
As the kite passes overhead, weight your front foot and edge your board in the direction of the turn. Your board will begin to rotate naturally. Your shoulders and hips follow your kite's movement.

Step 5: Swap Your Feet
This is the crucial moment. Release your back foot from the strap and pivot your body 180°. Your weight should shift from one side of the board to the other. Swap hands on the bar so your original back hand is now your front hand.

Step 6: Complete the Turn
As you come through the gyve, let the kite swing across and back down on the new side of the wind window. By now you should be riding away on your new tack. Immediately place your back foot in the rear strap and lock in your new stance.

Common Mistakes

  • Losing Kite Tension: Releasing the bar too early causes the kite to depower mid-turn. Stay connected to the bar and let it guide the rotation.
  • Insufficient Speed: Trying to gybe from a slow speed makes the manoeuvre harder. Build real momentum before you initiate.
  • Poor Foot Placement: Not swapping your feet cleanly leaves you twisted and unbalanced. Practice the foot swap repeatedly on land if needed.
  • Steering Too Early: Turning the board before the kite reaches overhead stalls the manoeuvre. Wait for the kite to be overhead first.
  • Neglecting Wind Window Awareness: Misjudging the wind window edges leads to over-rotation or under-rotation. Practise in moderate, consistent wind to build feel.

Surf Store Recommendation

Mastering how to gybe in kitesurfing is easier with the right kit. We stock responsive, forgiving kites from Duotone, Cabrinha, and NeilPryde that make learning gybes faster and safer. A wider, more stable board (like our JP Australia or Fanatic range) gives you the platform to focus purely on technique without worrying about balance.

For intermediate riders refining their gybes, we recommend directional twin-tips that respond quickly to weight shifts. Pair your setup with quality boots and a comfortable harness so you can concentrate on foot swaps and kite control. Visit surf-store.com to find the right board and kite for your level, and don't hesitate to contact our expert team—we can guide you on gear that supports smooth, controlled gybes.

title: How to Waterstart Windsurfing — Beginner Step-by-Step Guide
meta_title: How to Waterstart Windsurfing 2026 | Beginner Guide
meta_description: Master the waterstart in windsurfing with our step-by-step beginner guide. Learn technique, avoid common mistakes, and progress faster. Shop at Surf Store.
excerpt: A waterstart in windsurfing is when you launch into the wind from deep water by using the sail's power to lift you and the board onto the plane without needi...
body_html:
A waterstart in windsurfing is when you launch into the wind from deep water by using the sail's power to lift you and the board onto the plane without needing a beach. Once you've mastered how to waterstart windsurfing, you'll never need shallow water again, and you'll unlock the ability to sail longer sessions, reach deeper spots, and progress to advanced tricks. It's one of the most liberating skills in the sport.

The Full Answer

The waterstart is the gateway skill that separates beginner windsurfers from intermediate sailors. Instead of wading into shallow water, swimming the board into position, and standing up on the beach—a process that wears you out—you'll learn to let the wind and sail do the work for you while you're floating in deep water.

Here's what happens: you position your body in the water behind the board, grab the boom, and use the sail's power to pull you up and forward. As the board planes (lifts onto its sweet spot), your weight transfers onto it, and you're suddenly standing and moving. The technique relies on timing, balance, and understanding how wind pressure works. When done right, how to waterstart windsurfing becomes second nature within a few sessions of practice.

The beauty of this skill is that it transforms your entire windsurfing experience. You'll sail in areas where beach launches are impossible, extend your time on the water, and develop the confidence to handle stronger winds and bigger waters.

Practical Guide

Step 1: Position yourself correctly. Float behind the board with the sail lying flat on the water. Your feet should be towards the tail of the board, and the boom should be at chest height. Orient yourself so the nose of the board points toward the wind.

Step 2: Grab and rotate the sail. Hold the boom with both hands, slightly wider than shoulder-width apart. Slowly rotate the boom towards you so the sail catches wind and begins to pull you. Start gently—don't yank it; let the pressure build.

Step 3: Engage your core and body position. Keep your body low in the water and lean back slightly. Your arms should stay relatively straight but relaxed. As the sail fills, you'll feel the pull increase. Press your chest out against the boom rather than pulling with your arms.

Step 4: Pop onto the board. As the sail powers up, jump your feet underneath your body and onto the board. This isn't a violent motion—it's more of a scoop. Your weight should land over the centre of the board, not the tail.

Step 5: Bear away and plane. Once your feet are on the board, sheet the sail in (move the boom closer to your body) and shift your weight forward slightly. Point the nose downwind a touch so you can start moving. The board will plane, and you'll be sailing.

Common Mistakes

Pulling with your arms instead of using the sail's power. New sailors often try to haul themselves up by yanking the boom. The sail does the heavy lifting—your job is to position your body to receive that power.

Rotating the sail too fast. If you flip the sail suddenly, it catches too much wind at once, and you'll get pulled under. Smooth, deliberate rotation is the key to how to waterstart windsurfing successfully.

Jumping onto the board too early. Wait until the sail is truly powered and pulling. If you hop on too soon, the board won't have enough momentum to plane, and you'll sink.

Not keeping the board pointed at the wind initially. If the board angles downwind too early, the sail loses efficiency and the waterstart falls apart.

Leaning away from the boom. Press your chest forward into the sail, not backward. This keeps tension on the sail and ensures smooth power delivery.

Surf Store Recommendation

Learning how to waterstart windsurfing is so much easier when you have the right equipment. We stock boards from JP Australia and Fanatic with forgiving volume and rocker profiles that make waterstarts achievable. Sails from Duotone, NeilPryde, and Gaastra are built with the power delivery and consistency that make this skill accessible to beginners.

We also recommend picking up a wetsuit from ION to keep you warm during extended practice sessions—you'll want to drill this skill until it's automatic. Visit surf-store.com to explore our range of beginner-friendly boards and sails, and don't hesitate to contact our expert staff with questions about your setup.

title: How to Windsurf Faster — Pumping, Trim & Speed Techniques
meta_title: How to Windsurf Faster in 2026 | Pumping Technique
meta_description: Master pumping technique and speed secrets from our experts. Learn how to windsurf faster with proven methods. Shop gear at Surf Store.
excerpt: Windsurfing speed comes from efficient pumping technique, board control, and matching your kit to wind conditions.
body_html:
The fastest windsurfers aren't always those with the biggest sails—they're the ones who understand how to windsurf faster through efficient pumping technique, proper weight distribution, and reading the water. Speed in windsurfing depends on three core elements: your pumping rhythm, your stance on the board, and whether your equipment matches the conditions you're sailing.

The Full Answer

Pumping is the rhythmic movement of your body and arms that transfers energy into the sail and board, building momentum without relying on gusts. Unlike other water sports, windsurfing rewards technique over raw power. When you pump correctly, you're compressing and extending through your legs whilst timing your arm movements with the sail's natural flex, creating a propulsive wave that accelerates the board.

Speed in windsurfing also depends on your pumping technique working in harmony with board design. A board with good rocker (curve) and sufficient volume allows you to maintain planing more easily. Your sail choice matters too—a sail that's responsive and has good shape retention will reward your pumping efforts far more than a tired, baggy sail will.

Wind conditions play a role as well. Learning how to windsurf faster in marginal wind means mastering pumping; in stronger wind, it's about efficient carving, weight placement, and keeping the board flat or slightly heel-weighted depending on conditions.

Practical Guide

Step 1: Master the Pump Rhythm
Begin with your knees bent and your weight centred over the board. Push down into your legs (compression phase), then explosively extend upwards whilst pulling the boom towards your body with your arms. The timing should feel like a single, flowing motion—not two separate movements. Repeat this 4–6 times per cycle to build speed gradually.

Step 2: Position Your Weight Correctly
Beginners often stand too far back on the board, which kills speed. Stand with your feet roughly shoulder-width apart, centred laterally and positioned just slightly ahead of the mast. This balanced stance lets the board ride flat and respond to your pumping.

Step 3: Use Your Sail Efficiently
A responsive sail is essential for how to windsurf faster with pumping technique. Sails from brands like Duotone and NeilPryde are engineered with battens and materials that store and release energy well. Ensure your sail is properly rigged and the battens are tensioned correctly—a loose batten kills the pump.

Step 4: Choose the Right Board
In light wind, select a board with more volume and a wider stance zone. In moderate wind, a more conventional wave or freestyle board works better. Boards from JP Australia and Fanatic span these categories with design features that make pumping more rewarding.

Common Mistakes

  • Pumping too fast: Speed comes from rhythm and power, not frequency. Slow, controlled pumps are far more effective than frantic ones.
  • Standing too upright: This reduces leverage and tires you quickly. Keep your knees flexed and your centre of gravity low.
  • Ignoring sail trim: If your sail is over-sheeted or has loose battens, your pump won't translate into speed. Spend time getting your rig right.
  • Using worn equipment: An old sail or waterlogged board will never reward your technique as well as modern gear will.
  • Fighting the wind direction: Speed requires sailing at the angle the wind naturally wants to push you. Learn to read puffs and sail slightly downwind during your pump cycles.

Surf Store Recommendation

To master how to windsurf faster, invest in a modern sail and board combination. We recommend a Duotone Warp or NeilPryde Assassin sail paired with a JP Australia or Fanatic freestyle/wave board—both brands deliver the responsiveness and durability that make pumping rewarding. If you're sailing in lighter European conditions, look at our Point7 or Gaastra range, which excels in marginal wind.

Our expert staff at Surf Store have years of on-water windsurfing experience. If you're unsure which board or sail combination suits your style and local conditions, contact us at Surf Store—we'll match you with equipment that genuinely makes you faster. We ship across Europe in EUR and stand behind every recommendation with personal experience.

title: How to Duck Tack in Windsurfing — Advanced Technique Guide
meta_title: How to Duck Tack Windsurf: Advanced Technique 2026
